title: Energy-Efficient and Stable Task Offloading and Resource Allocation in Space-Air-Ground Integrated Networks_supp1-3668251.pdf
authors: Xiaoheng Deng
abstract: Task scheduling in sixth-generation (6G) space-air-ground integrated networks (SAGIN) faces fundamental challenges due to limited terrestrial infrastructure, time-varying wireless links, and stochastic task arrivals. In such dynamic environments, task offloading and resource scheduling decisions are strongly coupled over time through queue evolution, which makes it difficult to simultaneously guarantee service stability and energy efficiency. To address this issue, we formulate a long-term stochastic optimization problem that minimizes the average system energy consumption while stabilizing task queues. Leveraging Lyapunov drift-plus-penalty optimization, we develop an online Four-Phase Computation Offloading and Resource Scheduling (FCORS) algorithm that decomposes the per-slot control into four tractable subproblems and operates without any offline training. Simulation results demonstrate that FCORS achieves performance parity with state-of-the-art Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) baselines. Specifically, under heavy traffic loads, it reduces energy consumption by approximately 51.7 (QOA) while suppressing queue backlogs by approximately 90.4 (GEA). Therefore, FCORS provides a robust and lowcomplexity solution for energy-efficient task scheduling in future 6G SAGIN-enabled MEC systems.

title: MuST: Multi-Scale Transformer Incorporating Hierarchical Attention and TCN for EEG Decoding
authors: Kui Zhao, Enze Shi, Di Zhu, Sigang Yu, Geng Chen, Shijie Zhao, Dingwen Zhang, Shu Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) signals exhibit significant and inherent time scales differences across individuals and tasks. Despite notable successes in decoding EEG signals in single-tasks (e.g., detection of epilepsy), where the time scales are relatively consistent, substantial differences in temporal characteristics among various tasks pose a significant challenge. To address these limitations, we propose the MuST, which stands for Multi-Scale Transformer, aiming to dynamically learn characteristics of EEG signals on different time scales. Building on the conventional Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)-Transformer model, the MuST introduces two innovations: (1) A hierarchical Transformer structure to dynamically capture global dependencies and long-range information from EEG signals at different scales. (2) A novel temporal convolutional network (TCN) module to replace the original feed forward network (FFN) module in the Transformer, effectively capturing local temporal patterns and short-term dependencies from EEG signals. To validate the performance of the MuST, we conducted experiments on five public EEG datasets with extreme time-scale differences. The experimental results on these datasets demonstrate that we have achieved an average classification accuracy of 91.69% under identical parameter settings. This surpasses the baseline EEGNet by 5.65%, highlighting its superior capability in handling multi-scale EEG signals for diverse tasks. More critically, MuST demonstrates a successful unified modeling of EEG temporal heterogeneity through mixed dataset training (epilepsy detection and sleep staging classification). This breakthrough validates our multi-scale architecture's capability to dynamically reconcile divergent neurophysiological timescales within a single model. Our code can be found at https://github.com/wisercc/MuST.

title: Design and Implementation of Repetitive Control for Medium-Frequency Inverter Based on Double-Zero Approximation
authors: Zhuolin Yang, Qiang Qian, Xu Wang, Li Zhang, Shaojun Xie
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: The conventional implementation of the repetitive control (RC) in the standard 50/60 Hz inverter features fast roll-off of the gain-frequency response (GFR) at the frequency beyond the control bandwidth. Contributed by the high output frequency f0 of the medium frequency (MF) applications, the fine reshape of the RC plant at the frequency up to several kilohertz is nontrivial for enhancing the low-order harmonic rejection capability. Therefore, the implementation of RC is revisited from the perspective of structure selection, parameter tuning, and the trade-off between tracking performance and stability. Orienting at double-zero approximation over a wide frequency band, the design criteria and the optimizing principle of RC are elaborated for MF inverter. The plugged-in RC is employed, where the proportional-integral parameters are tuned by constraining the gain of the RC plant lower than 1. A biquad filter in series with the fractional-order zero-phase-shift (ZPS) filter is further used for the 0 dB approximation of the GFR of the RC plant, and the Lagrange interpolation-based phase-lead filter is adopted for 0-degree correction. Moreover, the damped ZPS filter-based internal model effectively avoids the center frequency shift and guarantees a high gain (>30 dB) at selected nf0. Finally, experiments are conducted on a 600 VA prototype, confirming that the dynamic and steady-state performance of the MF inverter are satisfactory with the implementation of the proposed RC.

title: Implementation of a Secure 32-bit RISC-V Microcontroller Integrated with a Fast Lightweight Post-Quantum NTRU-HPS Cryptosystem for IoT
authors: Tri-Duc Ta, Quoc-Thinh Tran, Cong-Kha Pham, Duc-Hung Le
abstract: The emergence of large-scale quantum computers threatens the long-term security of classical public-key cryptosystems, making the integration of post-quantum cryptography (PQC) essential for resource-constrained embedded and IoT platforms. To address this challenge, we propose a compact and secure RISC-V microcontroller tightly integrated with a lightweight, high-throughput NTRU-HPS-2048-509 hardware accelerator for quantum-resilient edge computing. Unlike recent PQC–RISC-V prototypes that focus only on software optimization or standalone polynomial engines, this work implements the complete NTRU KeyGen, encryption, and decryption pipeline entirely in hardware. A write-only, tamper-resistant interface ensures hardware-isolated and constant-time execution, while a precomputed Key-LUT removes runtime polynomial inversion, enabling fast and leakage-resistant key generation.Aparallel circular polynomial-multiplication engine combined with LUT-optimized modular arithmetic achieves single-loop N-cycle processing, enabling ultra-low-latency NTRU execution: 2,040 cycles for KeyGen, 2,039 cycles for encryption, and 3,058 cycles for decryption—achieving speedups of over 300 times (Enc) and 540 times (Dec) compared with optimized software implementations. The proposed SoC occupies only 14,019 adaptive logic modules (ALMs) on a Cyclone V SE FPGA, of which the NTRU accelerator accounts for 11,166 ALMs. In ASIC implementation using a 45 nm CMOS process, the SoC integrates 429,628 standard cells, with the NTRU accelerator contributing 122,382 cells (about 28.5% of SoC). Importantly, the total power consumption of the entire MCU is 59.26 mW at 160 MHz, and a compact 1.925 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> core area. Detailed power analysis reveals that 85% of this consumption is attributed to sequential and clock network overheads caused by implementing on-chip memory as standard cell registers. By isolating the computational logic, we estimate the NTRU accelerator's active power to be approximately 17.78 mW. We demonstrate that replacing register-based storage with SRAM hard macros is projected to reduce total system power by nearly 60% (to ~24.5 mW). The results confirm that while the accelerator logic is computationally intensive, the system's energy efficiency is sufficient for high-security IoT applications when optimized with dedicated memory macros. By combining RISC-V architectural openness with a lightweight, gate-efficient, and hardware-isolated PQC engine, thiswork establishes a practical, scalable blueprint for integrating lattice-based cryptography into next-generation IoT, edge-security systems.

title: CineSteady: A Real-time Hybrid Video Stabilization and Distortion Correction Method_supp4-3669217.mp4
authors: Heng Sun
abstract: The rapid development of the consumer electronics industry has highlighted the significance of video stabilization technologies for devices like smartphones. Nevertheless, State-of-the-Art (SOTA) solutions face significant challenges in realworld deployment, primarily due to limitations in stability, image distortion control, black border elimination, and real-time processing capabilities. To address these challenges, we propose CineSteady, a novel and comprehensive video stabilization algorithm framework. First, by using gyroscope data and image sequences, CineSteady achieves high-order smoothing of video sequences under complex motion scenarios, delivering a top-tier stabilization experience for users. In addition, we introduce a forward-backward filtering algorithm that rigorously enforces boundary constraints. This approach guarantees black-border-free output within a fixed Field of View (FOV) while maximizing video stability. Moreover, to address practical deployment challenges on mobile devices, we present Optical Lens-based Rolling Shutter Correction (OLRC), a comprehensive camera and distortion correction model. By leveraging physical imaging models and 3D motion estimation, OLRC effectively eliminates the jello effect. Finally, the proposed algorithm demonstrates exceptional computational efficiency, achieving real-time performance on the Xiaomi 15 Ultra powered by the Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Elite platform, supporting 4K resolution at 60 frames per second. Evaluations in diverse real-world scenarios confirm that CineSteady surpasses SOTA video stabilization methods in user experience, demonstrating significant advantages of the proposed solution.

title: DDFNet: Dual-Neighborhoods Dynamic Fusion Network for Image Feature Matching
authors: Changcai Yang, Fengyuan Zhuang, Xiaojie Li, Lifang Wei, Jiayi Ma, Riqing Chen
abstract: Establishing reliable correspondences is a fundamental task in computer vision. Constructing neighbor graphs in feature space with position information to mine correspondence consistency has become a common strategy for recognizing correct correspondences (inliers). However, these neighbors may include a high ratio of incorrect correspondences (outliers), only using the correspondence consistency from feature space will probably be difficult to guarantee the matching accuracy. To address this issue, we propose a novel motion consistent space to find consistent neighbors that are independent of the correspondence's position and have a larger search range. On top of that, we build two neighbor graphs according to the feature space and motion consistent space separately, and expand a shift annular convolution to retain rich neighbor graph structure information and fully exploit the neighborhood context. Then, we design a dynamic feature fusion block to dynamically fuse these dual-neighbor graphs to flexibly cope with various complex scenarios. Finally, we develop a Dual-Neighborhoods Dynamic Fusion Network (DDFNet) for accurately identifying inliers and retrieving camera poses. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed DDFNet outperforms the state-of-the-art methods. Source code: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/1211193023/DDFNet</uri>.

title: A Method for Generating Large-Scale Spaceborne Long-Wave Infrared Point-Target Images Using Landsat 8 TIRS Data
authors: Ruiyan Shan, Liquan Dong, Kang Li, Lianqing Dong, Guoxian Zheng, Muyao Zhang, Yu Wang, Weihe Ren
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Deep learning has demonstrated remarkable potential in spaceborne infrared small target detection; however, its application is severely bottlenecked by the scarcity of high-quality, annotated on-orbit data. To bridge this “Sim-to-Real” domain gap, this paper proposes a radiometrically consistent image generation framework capable of synthesizing large-scale, high-fidelity Long-Wave Infrared (LWIR) datasets. Unlike traditional methods that rely on simplistic image injection or computationally expensive ray-tracing, our approach adopts a hybrid strategy: it leverages the global archive of calibrated Landsat-8 data to ensure authentic background diversity and couples it with physically modeled targets derived from Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD). A key innovation is the Radiance-Domain Fusion mechanism, which integrates targets and backgrounds strictly in physical units considering atmospheric transmission and sensor degradation effects (PSF, motion blur, NEDT), thereby guaranteeing energy conservation for sub-pixel targets. Quantitative validation against real on-orbit imagery from the SDG satellite demonstrates that the proposed framework achieves superior fidelity, with intensity profiles and statistical metrics (HP-PSNR) closely matching real observations. This work provides a scalable solution to the data scarcity problem, offering a valuable foundation for training and benchmarking robust deep learning models in spaceborne surveillance tasks.

title:

authors: Hyunjin Shin, Gyuseong Kang, Yeseul Kim, Dohui Kim, Kyuseong Kim, Sunkyu Lee, Hangil Lee, Sanggyeong Won, Mijoung Kim, Jaeho Lee, Seungpil Ko, Jaechul Shim, Shinhee Han, Kiseok Suh, Sohee Hwang, Hyunchang Lee, Jonghoon Jung, Sanghoon Baek
abstract: This paper proposes techniques to improve read margin and write performance for an 8nm eMRAM: temperature compensation, resistance-mismatch improvement and margin-verify-write MVW. These techniques enable 8ns read and 300ns write operations for 283b at a time with a 128Mb density in a temperature range of −40 −150°C and from a 0.60V supply. A 19.94Mb/mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> memory macro is implemented and silicon validation shows a mass-production yield is achieved.

title: Transferable Pre-training and Transformer-based Fewshot Learning for One Dimensional Radar Range Profile Target Recognition
authors: Cai Yu, Yingying Kong
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computer Networks and Communications (SPCNC)
abstract: Radar high resolution range profiles (HRRPs) offer rich structural information and are easy to acquire, making them valuable for radar target recognition. However, when the overall dataset is small, inter-class differences are subtle, and intra-class variations are large, traditional models struggle to achieve stable and generalizable recognition performance. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a few-shot inference framework that integrates transferable pre-training with Transformer-based metric learning. First, a pyramid-style convolutional backbone is designed and pre-trained on radar datasets using a transferable learning strategy, enabling multi-scale feature extraction and producing robust feature representations even under limited data conditions. Subsequently, during episodic meta-training, the backbone parameters are reused and optimized within various few-shot tasks, allowing the model to adapt efficiently across different task configurations. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves stronger generalization for ship targets of previously unseen aspect categories under few-shot settings, and significantly outperforms mainstream baselines in both recognition accuracy and recall.

title: Design of a High-Speed ADC Chip Testing System
authors: Ziyun Jiang, Renjie Su, Xin Chen
publish: 2025 7th International Conference on Intelligent Control, Measurement and Signal Processing (ICMSP)
abstract: With the continuous evolution of ultra-high-speed time-interleaved chips, the intelligence and reliability of chip testing have become increasingly critical. This paper presents an intelligent test system for an ultra-high-speed time-interleaved ADC chip. The system integrates one-stop operations, including intelligent data acquisition with human intervention, adaptive calibration range selection, and performance index comparison. On the hardware side, a self-developed PCB with high-speed interfaces ensures stable input of sampling signals and clocks, while an FPGA facilitates UART and SPI communication for data upload to a host PC. On the software side, it incorporates COM instrument control and DSP calibration modules to achieve adaptive correction of offset and gain errors. Finally, the system provides a human-machine interface for waveform display and performance analysis, delivering a comprehensive intelligent testing solution.

title: A 3.19pJ/b Electro-Optical Router with 18ns Setup Frame-Level Routing and 1-to-6 Wavelength-Flexible Link Capacity for Photonic Interposers
authors: Yvain Thonnart, Christian Bernard, Stephane Bernabe, Myriam Assous, Laura Boutafa, Benoit Charbonnier, Remi Franiatte, Cesar Fuguet, Olivier Guille, Vincent Josselin, Stephane Malhouitre, Laurent Mendizabal, Andre Myko, Damien Saint Patrice, Remi Velard, Jean Charbonnier
abstract: Global interconnect on interposers between chiplets is currently mostly limited to nearest-neighbor communication. We propose an electro-optical router in 28nm on a photonic interposer to handle queuing and routing between dies and optical link drivers capable of 18ns link setup time for frame-level routing, with 1-to-6 lambda flexible wavelength capacity. Compact analog drivers and standard-cell-based SerDes and clocking achieve 0.007mm2 active area per link and 3.19pJ/b power.

title: Data-Driven SCUC Based on GRU and Seq2Seq
authors: Chi Zhang, Linghao Li, Zhi Cai
abstract: To address the insufficient decision-making accuracy of data-driven unit commitment models, this paper proposes a novel approach based on GRU and Seq2Seq. First, the training sample matrix is preprocessed using sample encoding techniques. Second, a composite neural network architecture with Encoder-Decoder structure is constructed by employing GRU as neurons and integrating Seq2Seq, thereby developing a deep learning model specifically for unit commitment decision- making. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed method is verified through IEEE 118-bus test case simulations.

title: An Experimental Study on Vibration Characteristics of Mechanical Faults in a Single-Phase Dual-Gapped-Limb Reactor
authors: Linggang Zhou, Xu Jiang, Fei He, Zhiming Zhang, Ze Zong, Lingzhi Li
publish: 2025 6th International Symposium on New Energy and Electrical Technology (ISNEET)
abstract: Ultra high voltage single-phase dual-gapped-limb shunt reactors are extensively utilized in power systems. While analyzing their enclosure vibration signals enables fault diagnosis, directly simulating mechanical faults and analyzing vibration characteristics under fault conditions remains challenging. To address this, a novel vibration experiment simulation platform for single-phase dual-gapped-limb shunt reactors was developed, providing a novel method for controlled vibration analysis. This study simulates mechanical faults caused by varying degrees of the gapped limb clamping loosening and analyzes the acquired vibration signals. Through time-domain waveforms, frequency spectra, and continuous wavelet transform maps, the evolution of vibration signals during progressive gapped limb looseness was investigated. Additionally, the patterns of vibrational spectral entropy across different measurement points were systematically summarized, providing experimental evidence for subsequent fault diagnosis in single-phase dual-gapped-limb shunt reactors. The comparison of vibration signals under different fault conditions further highlights the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

title: PIN Diode-Based RF Limiter Topologies for Multifunction Receiver Protection
authors: Will M. Vu, Kenneth E. Kolodziej
abstract: Delicate components in the front ends of sensitive RF receivers are vulnerable to damage from high-powered signals. RF limiters are crucial in protecting components downstream of the receive antenna by attenuating high-amplitude inputs to safe levels while maintaining receiver sensitivity and dynamic range. This paper presents the characterization of eight limiters in passive, quasi-active, and active topologies. The gain, output power, and OIP3 of each limiter is measured with a −10 to 40 dBm input at 1 GHz. The results demonstrate that these designs and their cascade into multi-stage structures can provide a high-power tolerant and adjustable RF limiter with flexible performance required for receivers in integrated sensing and communications (ISAC) applications.

title: Approximate Multiplier Using Approximate Compressor and Parallel Prefix Adder
authors: E. Pavithra, Gaddam Manisha, Adarsh K S, Kambham Vishnu Vardhan Reddy
publish: 2025 IEEE First International Conference on Innovations in Engineering and Next-Generation Technologies for Sustainability (ICINVENTS)
abstract: Approximate computing provides an effective solution to increase performance and reduce power consumption in digital systems where a certain degree of inaccuracy can be tolerated. Multipliers, due to their highpower consumption and area, are natural candidates for approximation. This paper presents an approximate unsigned multiplier based on a multi-level approximate compressor and a parallel prefix Kogge-Stone adder in order to increase computational efficiency. It employs inexact logic to implement lower order bits and exact logic to implement higher order bits, thereby achieving a subtle balance between hardware efficiency and accuracy. Though the approach demonstrates considerable improvements in terms of speed, power, and silicon area compared to standard designs, novelty compared to known approximate multiplier architectures appears to be minimal. Nonetheless, the design provides real benefits in error-tolerating application areas, such as image processing and neural networks, where negligible inaccuracies in computation are permitted. Simulation and synthesis results verify its potential to sustain reasonable levels of accuracy while delivering measurable hardware performance improvements.

title: Research on Dynamic Characteristic Optimization of PV-Storage Integrated Distribution Network Under Extreme Weather Based on GNN-PPO
authors: Chen Liu, Changjian Si, Chunming Zhao, Naiyu Liu, Daiyong Yang
publish: 2025 6th International Symposium on New Energy and Electrical Technology (ISNEET)
abstract: Aiming at the problem that extreme weather causes violent fluctuations in photovoltaic (PV) output and sudden changes in load-side demand, which in turn leads to the deterioration of dynamic characteristics such as voltage deviation and frequency fluctuation in the PV-energy storage (ESS) integrated distribution network, a dynamic characteristic regulation algorithm (GNN-PPO) integrating Graph Neural Network (GNN) and Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) is proposed. Firstly, a distribution network topology model including distributed PV and ESS is constructed, and the deep correlation features between node states and network topology under extreme weather are mined through GNN. Secondly, the state input and policy output modules of the PPO algorithm are improved to realize the collaborative decision-making of ESS charging/discharging power and PV output smoothing. Finally, simulations are carried out based on the IEEE 33-node system and typical extreme weather data. The results show that the GNN-PPO algorithm can control the absolute value of voltage deviation within 3% and narrow the frequency fluctuation range to ±0.1Hz. Compared with the traditional PID control and the single PPO algorithm, the voltage regulation accuracy is improved by 22.6%-31.8%, and the frequency recovery speed is accelerated by more than 40%, which effectively enhances the dynamic stability of the distribution network under extreme weather.

title: Reverse Antenna Effect: A Hidden Reliability Risk in BEOL Interconnects
authors: Olga Keplinger, Rameez Raja Shaik, Michael Zier, Henning Turnow, Kiril Borisov, Michael Zschornack, Manjunatha Prabhu
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Emerging Electronics (ICEE)
abstract: Plasma-induced damage (PID) is a major cause of yield loss in standard semiconductor fabrication, typically driven by plasma charging during dielectric or metal etching process. This kind of damage usually hits devices with gate dielectrics, where accumulated charge leads to dielectric breakdown. To prevent it, design rules based on metal-to-gate area ratios are applied. This study examines a less explored failure mode known as reverse antenna charging. Here, the damage doesn’t occur at the gate dielectric but within the back-end-of-line (BEOL) interconnects, away from the front-end-of-line (FEOL) devices. Phenomenon was first observed in product and then test structures were built for thick oxide gate dielectric devices through a common BEOL interconnect path strategy. Gates were routed through CA, M1, V1, M2, V2, and M3 layers, with failures observed at the M1-V1 levels for structures connected to thick gate devices when discharge paths were intentionally designed to be weak. Under these conditions, reverse charging effects were successfully isolated and reproduced, resulting in hard open failures. The results confirm that yield loss can still occur even when thick gate devices are designed according to existing antenna rule strategies. This points to a failure mechanism that current rules did not address. As a result, a need is identified for existing design strategies to be revised to account for reverse antenna charging and its impact on BEOL interconnect reliability.

title: Deciphering the Influence of Human Behavior on Cybersecurity Risks: An In-depth Review of Amplification and Mitigation Factors
authors: Fine Masimba, Faith Gumbo, Tranos Zuva
publish: 2025 5th International Multidisciplinary Information Technology and Engineering Conference (IMITEC)
abstract: Human behavior remains one of the most critical yet unpredictable elements influencing cyber security risks. This review paper explores how individual and organizational behaviors can either amplify or mitigate cyber threats. Drawing upon interdisciplinary literature from cyber security, psychology and organizational studies, the paper identifies key behavioral patterns such as negligence, social engineering susceptibility and poor security culture that exacerbate vulnerabilities. Conversely, it examines how awareness training, user-centered design, behavioral monitoring and positive reinforcement mechanisms contribute to risk mitigation. By synthesizing current research and case studies, the paper provides a comprehensive framework for understanding the behavioral dynamics in cyber security, offering practical recommendations for policymakers, system designers and security professionals aiming to strengthen humancentric security strategies.

title: Session 36 Overview: Neural and Biomedical Interfaces
authors: N/A

title: Empowering the next Wave of Silicon Engineers
authors: Hope Giles
abstract: Developing custom chips for specific applications enables fundamentally better products. As part of a full-stack optimization approach to product design, application-specific systems-on-chips (SoCs) have been one of the keys to delivering game-changing performance, power efficiency, capabilities, and user experiences. This plenary provides examples of how custom silicon enables innovative product breakthroughs and how techniques like design modularity help manage the SoC scaling challenge. As applications, such as artificial intelligence (AI), transform both silicon demand and design methodologies, we need to reimagine how we motivate and educate the next generation of silicon engineers to address these challenges. We will share our experiences in interactions between industry, universities, and students, showing how industry engagement can re-energize these programs. The future of custom silicon depends on all of us investing in the next wave of engineers who will create tomorrow's innovative products.

title: Comparative Analysis of PI, Fractional PI, and ILC PI Controllers for Liquid Level Regulation in Conical Tank
authors: Nagarajapandian M., Anitha T., Arun Mozhi Devan P., Rukkumani V., Raksha G.K., Sabarisri S., Sarmitha S.
publish: 2025 First International Conference of Advances in Engineering and Computing Technologies for Sustainable Development (AECTSD)
abstract: Many complex systems consist of multiple interacting and non-interacting loops, exhibiting characteristics such as linearity, non-linearity, stability, and time variance. Ensuring effective control is essential for accurate set-point tracking and disturbance rejection. This study focuses on the level control of a non-linear conical tank system, modelled as a Single Input Single Output (SISO) process. Due to its inherent non-linearity, advanced control strategies are required to enhance system performance using controller design. Three control strategies PI Controller, Fractional Order PI Controller, and Iterative Learning Control (ILC) PI Controller are implemented in MATLAB, and their performance is compared graphically. The proposed ILC-PI controller shows superior performance by accurately reaching set-points 13, 33, and 48 with minimal overshoot and fast settling. It maintains control signals below 250%, 6000%, and 7000% respectively, while effectively rejecting disturbances around 50s. Compared to FOPI and PI, ILC-PI ensures better stability and quicker recovery. The findings contribute to improved process control in non-linear systems and can be extended to industrial applications requiring precise liquid level regulation.

title: Single-Phase Open-Circuit Fault Location Method Based on Graph Attention Network
authors: Hongcheng Nie
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Systems, Smart Grid, and Artificial Intelligence (PSGAI)
abstract: To achieve single-phase open-circuit fault location in distribution networks, a graph attention network algorithm is proposed. This method introduces a multi-head attention mechanism on the basis of Graph Convolutional Networks, where each attention head performs differentiated aggregation of neighboring node information through an adaptive weight distribution mechanism. The core of this mechanism lies in that nodes compute attention coefficients with their neighboring nodes via learnable weight parameters, enabling dynamic weight allocation based on the relevance of node features. The feature vectors output by each head are concatenated or averaged. Finally, a simulation model based on a typical 10 kV distribution network structure is constructed. The proposed method achieves an average localization accuracy exceeding 95%, and under conditions of noise and data loss, the localization error distance remains within 200 meters. This work provides an effective datadriven solution for fault location in complex distribution networks, contributing to the enhancement of reliability and intelligence in power grid operation and maintenance.

title: A Method for Estimating the State of Charge of a Vanadium Redox Flow Battery with SSA-BiLSTM Architecture Under Constant Power
authors: Xi Wang, Changxin Chen, Yifeng Ren, Wei Jia, Yinxia Gao, Yin Jie Wang, Chao Xu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Systems, Smart Grid, and Artificial Intelligence (PSGAI)
abstract: Vanadium redox flow batteries (VRFB) possess advantages such as high safety, long cycle life, and environmental friendliness, showing broad application prospects in areas such as grid peak shaving and renewable energy utilization. However, in actual operation, exhibiting significant nonlinearity, time-varying parameters, and environmental sensitivity, making it difficult to accurately estimate their state of charge (SOC). To address these issues, this paper leverages the advantages of machine learning models in characterizing nonlinearity and uncertainty, constructing an SOC estimation model based on a bidirectional long short-term memory (BiLSTM) network, and introducing a Sparrow Search Algorithm (SSA) to globally optimize the network hyperparameters. BiLSTM, through its bidirectional information transfer mechanism, fully utilizes the forward and backward time dependencies in the SOC evolution process. The SSA-BiLSTM model constructed based on this method achieves high-precision SOC estimation for VRFB. Experimental results show that the SSA-BiLSTM network outperforms the independent BiLSTM network in all three metrics. After optimization using the SSA, the batch size was 32, the number of units was 64, the learning rate was 2.73847077702087e-3, the coefficient of determination <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{2}$</tex> reached 0.9448, the root mean square error was reduced to 3.4993%, and the mean absolute error was reduced to 3.1453%. The method showed high estimation accuracy and stability under experimental conditions, providing an effective technical means and a new research direction for improving the performance of vanadium redox flow battery management systems. Future work will focus on improving the model's response time and robustness, addressing challenges in real-time application and noise sensitivity for enhanced performance in practical scenarios.

title: A Multi-Range, Multi-Resolution LiDAR Sensor with 2,880-Channel Modular Survival Histogramming TDC and Delay Compensation Using Double Histogram Sampling
authors: Minkyung Kim, Junsang Bae, Donghyuk Kim, Jung-Hoon Chun, Seong-Jin Kim, Jaehyuk Choi
abstract: We present a 720 192dToF sensor with a 2,880-channel modular survival histogram TDC (shTDC) array. The shTDC supports non-destructive accumulation while concentrating depth on active bins, reducing memory by 75% with precision comparable to one-step hTDC. Modular hTDCs enable multi-range and multi-resolution imaging to 69.6 m, while double histogram sampling cancels pixel-to-shTDC offsets. The prototype achieves 0.13% precision and 0.12% accuracy.

title: Analysis of die-lamination process during epoxy-die-attach
authors: Ridham Jignesh Shah, Anil Raj Purra, Zi Hao Chong, Siva Sai Kishore Palli, Venkata Rama Satya Pradeep Vempaty
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Emerging Electronics (ICEE)
abstract: In semiconductor manufacturing, the epoxy die-attach process plays a pivotal role in ensuring the mechanical integrity, thermal management, and electrical performance of silicon-based devices, particularly in Board on Chip (BOC) configurations. This study investigates the five-step bonding sequence involving laser marking for traceability, stencil-based epoxy dispensing, B-stage curing, die-lamination, and final C-stage curing. The epoxy adhesive not only secures the die to the substrate but also facilitates heat dissipation and electrical conductivity. However, bonding reliability is critically influenced by filleting parameters—epoxy spread, coverage, and climb—especially at die edges and corners. Observations during die-lamination reveal corner non-fill and non-uniform epoxy distribution, which can lead to delamination and cracking post mold curing. The current work focuses on evaluating the impact of compression speed, epoxy print position, and stencil design on filleting quality. Additionally, the study simulates void entrapment scenarios during the printing process, analyzing the behavior of voids located at the die edge, corner, and mold center. These simulations provide insights into void removal dynamics and their potential expansion or contraction during curing. The findings aim to optimize stencil geometry and process parameters to improve die-attach reliability and reduce failure rates in high-performance semiconductor packages. This research contributes to advancing robust packaging technologies essential for modern electronics.

title: Ethical Considerations in AI-Driven Surveillance Systems
authors: Charan Thumma, Abhignan Srivatsava Sribhashyam, Saicharan Kadari, Manisha Bhende, Himanshu Sharma, Srinivas Aluvala
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Network Systems (CINS)
abstract: Surveillance technology is increasingly employed both in private and public places with enhanced surveillance capacity, but with very grave ethical concerns, one of the significant among them being invasion of privacy, discriminatory algorithms, and having no mechanism of having sufficient legal accountability. These issues are conveniently brushed under the carpet due to technological advances at a breakneck speed, and that is endangering marginalized groups and civil liberties. This paper presents a novel, measurable Ethical Risk Score (ERS) model based on ethical theory mapping, mathematical risk modeling, and case analysis. Compared to previous work on intangible ethics, this model is measurable and scalable in terms of its capacity to assess AI surveillance activity. The application of a utilitarian argument is established in empirical evidence in the world at large, the most dangerous application of facial recognition technology to policing. Analytic insights are translated into policy and design implications for policy relevance. Findings indicate that Facial Recognition in Policing had the highest Ethical Risk Score at 77.6%, and Retail Consumer Tracking had the lowest score at 45.0%, with context-specific regulation coupled with ethical protection.

title: Session 34 Overview: Integrated Radar and UWB Transceivers from Microwave to Sub-THz
authors: N/A

title: Comparison of Performance Metrics of Various ML-based Load Balancing Methods using a Benchmarked Dataset
authors: Nisha Devi, Sandeep Dalal
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Network Systems (CINS)
abstract: In cloud computing systems, load balancing has emerged as a critical concept for resource management and optimization. Load balancing and resource allocation in cloud data centers have become extremely challenging due to the widespread use of IoT devices, high costs, and increased energy consumption. Simultaneously, the growing application of machine learning and deep learning has introduced novel approaches to address these issues. This paper discusses the problems associated with load balancing and load prediction at the data center level. We have examined various machine learning and deep learning methodologies that are effective in addressing these challenges. We have also endeavored to replicate the performance of some widely employed ML-DL approaches, namely SVM, DQN, NN, and A2C, in cloud computing by utilizing a benchmarked dataset sourced from the GitHub community, based on metrics such as execution time, Degree of Imbalance (DoI), fault tolerance, and energy consumption, by simulating a cloud environment using Python. The simulation results show that DQN outperforms the other techniques in terms of execution time, DoI, and task assignment. It also outperformed A2C and NN in terms of energy consumption.

title: Modified Sepic Converter Topology for Efficient Solar-Powered Fast Ev Charging
authors: Hema R
publish: 2025 IEEE First International Conference on Innovations in Engineering and Next-Generation Technologies for Sustainability (ICINVENTS)
abstract: The global increase of energy demand, increases the fossil fuel reserves depletion, environmental and sustainability challenges. Slow charging infrastructure and a strong reliance on traditional power sources prevent electric vehicles from becoming widely used, even though they are increasingly seen as a viable answer. This paper proposes a modified SEPIC Converter technology in conjunction with a KY boost converter to design and develop a solar-powered EV rapid charging gateway. To maximize power extraction under different irradiance conditions, the solar array is interfaced with an MPPT controller. Using MATLAB/Simulink platform, a solar input of 24 V was effectively increased to 510 V, allowing for effective EV battery charging. The converter achieves a voltage gain significantly higher with continuous input current, and reduced ripples. The charging process follows a two-phase constant current-constant voltage (CC-CV) strategy to reduce a charging time by more than half while preserving battery health. The outcomes confirm that renewable-powered fast charging is both technically feasible and environmentally sustainable, and future work will focus on hardware implementation and smart-grid integration.

title: Applications and Research Challenges of Using Virtual Reality (VR) in Education
authors: Yarra Krishna Teja, V. M. Manikandan
publish: 2025 IEEE First International Conference on Innovations in Engineering and Next-Generation Technologies for Sustainability (ICINVENTS)
abstract: Education is essential for our lives, but most people are not aware of the different kinds of education available to us and have limited knowledge about the technologies that can contribute to education. In this article, we discuss the various types of education and how advanced technologies like smart classrooms and virtual reality can help students gain knowledge in an easier way. We also explore the applications of virtual reality in education and provide some platforms for developing virtual reality content. Various research papers that help in understanding virtual reality and its role in education are described. We also discuss the different types and models of VR headsets along with their specifications, which can be used for educational purposes. Finally, we examine the research challenges associated with using VR in education.

title: Arm Vein with Occlusion Recognition Algorithm Based on Dual-Branch Network
authors: Chen Yang, Chaoying Tang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computer Networks and Communications (SPCNC)
abstract: This paper addresses the issue of venous feature loss caused by local occlusion in arm vein recognition by proposing a dual-branch network-based method for local occlusion arm vein recognition. The method employs a global feature extraction branch combined with a dual attention calibration mechanism to suppress interference from occluded regions and focus on effective features in non-occluded regions. Simultaneously, a local branch is designed to extract detailed features, and a cross-attention mechanism is utilized to achieve complementary fusion of global and local features. To further enhance the model’s discriminative ability, a dynamic additive angular margin loss is introduced, which adaptively adjusts the angular boundaries between categories, improving the distinction and generalization performance for similar categories. A local occlusion dataset is constructed based on existing arm vein images. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a Rank-1 recognition rate of 78.8%, outperforming traditional methods and mainstream deep learning models, validating its effectiveness and advancement in arm vein identity recognition tasks under occlusion conditions.

title: Intra-Pulse Motion Compensation for Plasma-Sheath-Covered Targets
authors: Yixuan Song, Chongyi Fan, Shuang Yue, Yishi Li, Xiaotao Huang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computer Networks and Communications (SPCNC)
abstract: ISAR imaging for hypersonic targets is of significant importance and remains a challenging task. Due to their extreme flight velocities, hypersonic vehicles generate plasma sheath on their surface, and induce shift and broadening in the range profiles, thus severely degrading the quality of ISAR imaging results. To address this issue, this paper proposes an intra-pulse motion compensation algorithm that integrates the CLEAN technique with minimum image entropy method. Firstly, the spatial distribution characteristics of the plasma sheath are analyzed, and an ISAR echo model for targets enveloped by the plasma sheath is established. Then, the CLEAN technique is employed to extract the echo of each individual scattering point sequentially, and the velocity of scattering points is estimated and compensated using the minimum image entropy method. The proposed method effectively overcomes the challenges posed by different coupling velocities at various scattering points and achieves accurate velocity estimation. Simulation results demonstrate the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed algorithm.

title: Design and Performance Analysis of HexTorus Topology in NoC Architectures
authors: Jiasen Du, Meaad Fadhel, Cheru Haile
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computer Networks and Communications (SPCNC)
abstract: This paper presents the HexTorus topology for Network-on-Chip (NoC) architectures, addressing bottlenecks found in traditional Mesh and Honeycomb designs. As multicore systems become increasingly complex and demand highperformance communication, there is a pressing need for efficient interconnect topologies. The HexTorus topology, with its regular and highly interconnected structure, significantly enhances performance by improving diameter, boosting throughput, reducing delay, and enhancing packet delivery efficiency. Using a modified Dijkstra algorithm tailored to our network constraints, simulations show that HexTorus outperforms Honeycomb-based topologies across key metrics while transmitting the same number of packets. Specifically, throughput increases by $44 \%$ compared to the Honeycomb topology under identical load conditions.

title: Research on Deep Learning-Based Fault Diagnosis Methods for Steady-State Operating Conditions
authors: Honglin Pan, Mengfan Wang, Yunze Xue, Ke Su, Yizhuo Li, Bowen Zhang
publish: 2025 7th International Conference on Intelligent Control, Measurement and Signal Processing (ICMSP)
abstract: This study modifies the structure of existing fault diagnosis models by integrating the strengths of different algorithms to construct a unique diagnostic framework. Rather than performing direct fault diagnosis, this method employs a prediction-then-diagnosis workflow to identify faults based on residuals. The model first employs deep learning algorithms to train parameters under normal operating conditions, forming a prediction model. Subsequently, actual data from fault conditions is input into the model to generate predicted values. These predicted values are compared with the actual fault data to produce residuals, which are then used to classify faults. Model validation results demonstrate that the LSTM prediction model maintains stable prediction values during faults. Residuals differ across various fault conditions, enabling the classification model to accurately identify distinct fault types based on residuals between predicted and actual values. Classification accuracy reaches 97.67%, validating the method's effectiveness and feasibility.

title: Interests Balancing Evolutionary Strategy for Investors and Governments in Hydrogen-Electricity Coupling System
authors: Kai Wang, Zhimei Cao, Chengjie Liu, Zhengkui Zhao, Zhe Wang, Xiaoling Su
publish: 2025 6th International Symposium on New Energy and Electrical Technology (ISNEET)
abstract: Hydrogen-electric coupling systems, as a pivotal pathway for clean energy transition, have been significantly influenced by the strategic interaction between investors and governments. To address the issue of unequal interests of the two groups, this paper constructs an evolutionary game model between investors in hydrogen-electric coupling systems and the government, which analyzes the dynamic evolution of both parties in investment decisions and policy support. It is clear that investors' decisions are affected by their anticipated sales revenue, government subsidy incentives, and the cost of investment; while government policies depend on the cost of subsidies and the social benefits. To validate the strategic intentions of various entities, the model is assigned numerical values based on realistic conditions, by replicating dynamic equations to solve equilibrium points, while based on simulation models, it was verified that the government should ultimately favor supportive policies, with investors heavily investing in hydrogen-electric coupling systems as the evolutionarily stable equilibrium point. This research provides theoretical foundations and decision-making references for investors and governments in future production choices and policy formulation.

title: Energy-Aware Cluster Head Selection in IoT Using Logarithmic Mean Optimization
authors: Praveen R, R Baskar, R M Suganeshwaran, S Bharath Kumar
publish: 2025 First International Conference of Advances in Engineering and Computing Technologies for Sustainable Development (AECTSD)
abstract: Energy efficiency and network lifetime are major challenges in Internet of Things (IoT) networks because sensor nodes operate on limited battery capacity and replacing them frequently is costly and impractical. This paper presents an energy-aware cluster head selection method based on the Logarithmic Mean-Based Optimization (LMO) algorithm to achieve balanced energy consumption and reliable data transmission. The proposed LMO-based scheme selects cluster heads by jointly considering residual energy, communication cost, and node distribution. Simulation results demonstrate that the method extends network longevity by delaying the first node death to 600 rounds, compared to 480 rounds achieved by LEACH. At round 600, the LMO approach maintains an average residual energy of 1.05 J, whereas LEACH retains only 0.65 J. Additionally, LMO achieves a packet delivery ratio of 95.4% and a throughput of 1530 packets, outperforming GA, PSO, ACO, GWO, CSA, FA, and LEACH. The algorithm also maintains moderate communication overhead of approximately 220 control messages and converges more rapidly to superior fitness values, confirming its effectiveness and scalability for energy-aware clustering in dynamic IoT environments.

title: NEST: A Low-Cost Smart Hub for Environmental Monitoring and Forecasting in Poultry Broiler Houses
authors: Nico Steyn, Duanne Engelbrecht, Gustave Udahemuka, Karim Djouani
publish: 2025 5th International Multidisciplinary Information Technology and Engineering Conference (IMITEC)
abstract: This paper presents NEST (Network-Edge Sensing and Tracking), a compact, low-cost, and low-power platform developed for real-time environmental monitoring and forecasting in poultry broiler houses. NEST captures key parameters such as temperature, humidity, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CO}_{2}$</tex> across multiple zones using wireless sensors and aggregates data via LoRa and Wi-Fi communication protocols. To enable proactive management of broiler house conditions, the system employs a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model trained on historical sensor data to forecast live broiler weight. These forecasts support timely interventions, improving animal welfare and energy efficiency. Designed for standalone operation, NEST also integrates Instant Messaging (IM) capabilities to alert users about abnormal patterns or predicted thresholds, supporting swift decision-making. The name “NEST” reflects both the system's integration into poultry environments and its role as a data aggregation ‘nest’ for intelligent monitoring and forecasting.

title: Unsupervised Lemon Quality Segmentation Using ResNet50 and Spectral Clustering
authors: Tinashe Crispen Garidzira, Nobert Jere
publish: 2025 5th International Multidisciplinary Information Technology and Engineering Conference (IMITEC)
abstract: The visual quality of citrus fruits like lemons is an important indicator in post-harvest grading and market value determination. This study presents an unsupervised approach for clustering lemon images using deep feature extraction and Spectral Clustering adapting the Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining methodology. High-level embeddings were obtained from raw lemon images with a pretrained ResNet50, capturing important characteristics such as texture, color and blemishes. Spectral Clustering with a nearest-neighbors affinity and K-Means labeling segmented the images into three quality clusters which was then visualized using t-SNE. Evaluation with clustering metric such as Silhouette Score (0.153), Davies-Bouldin Index (2.027) and Calinski-Harabasz Index (287.165) demonstrated effective separation of lemon qualities. The findings suggest that combining deep Convolutional Neural Networks features with unsupervised clustering provides a scalable solution for automated quality assessment in agriculture, reducing reliance on manual grading and annotated datasets. This approach reduces reliance on hand-crafted features and can scale effectively for real-time deployment in citrus grading systems.

title: Alibi: A Graph-Driven Algorithm for Attendance Capturing
authors: Shaahid Rawat, Abejide Ade-Ibijola
publish: 2025 5th International Multidisciplinary Information Technology and Engineering Conference (IMITEC)
abstract: Class attendance has been an instrument for measuring students' commitment to learning over the years. Educators often rely on capturing class attendance as it allows them to pinpoint under-performing students, determine the effectiveness of classes, and understand the root causes of chronic absenteeism using techniques such as traditional attendance forms or online portals. With the advancements in artificial intelligence and other new technologies, new techniques such as facial recognition have also been applied in attendance capturing. All techniques used for these tasks have one or more drawbacks (such as easy manipulation, ineffectiveness, increase administration efforts, untraceable attendance or missing attendance data), hence, there is a consistent need to device new and more effective techniques of capturing class attendances. In this paper we have proposed a new technique/algorithm called Alibi; inspired by the act of finding alibis in crime investigations. Alibi uses the proximity of students to construct undirected graphs and automatically implies which students are present at the location. We have implemented Alibi in two applications: one mobile application that students can use to find Alibis in the classroom, and the other is a web application that provides a dashboard to the instructor for managing their class attendances. We have evaluated Alibi and its effectiveness amongst students and educators, both categories of users stated that this is a very useful tool and technique for attendance capturing. Out of 101 students, 90.1 % of which suggested that they would use Alibi as an attendance capturing system due to it's ease of use and simplicity. The four educators who interacted with the tool also expressed a positive sentiment.

title: Efficient Transfer Learning for Time-Series Forecasting Using Wi-Fi Data •
authors: Raghav Chandna, Phodiso Maroeshe, Manal Karmoude, Phuthi Rapheeha, Bevan Smith, Bruce Mellado
publish: 2025 5th International Multidisciplinary Information Technology and Engineering Conference (IMITEC)
abstract: Transfer learning has been gaining significant traction as a means of reusing representations learned in one domain to accelerate and improve performance in another. While it first rose to prominence in Natural Language Processing and Computer Vision, it is increasingly being recognized as a powerful framework for other domains, including time-series analysis. In this work, we investigate transfer learning for medium to long horizon time-series forecasting of Wi-Fi traffic across building floors. We train a seasonality-aware model on one floor (source domain) and transfer them to other floors (target domains) to reduce data and compute requirements while preserving accuracy. Our approach decouples seasonal structure by first modeling recurring seasonal patterns using the Seasonal Auto-Regressive Integrated Moving Average (SARIMA) model then learn the trend and residuals with an LSTM. We implement the transfer model with partial fine-tuning strategies, and evaluate performance using accuracy and number of model parameters as key metrics. In addition, we compare the fine-tuned models against baseline models trained from scratch to assess how closely transfer learning approaches approximate full training. The proposed transfer learning framework is easily scalable to larger sensor networks, particularly in air-quality monitoring systems, requiring only minimal re-training. Moreover, it offers greater efficiency than knowledge distillation, which depends on first training a large, high-parameter teacher model.

title: A Computer Vision-Based System for Detecting Eating Activities in Public Bus Transportation
authors: Ovie Michael Odafe, Witesyavwirwa Vianney Kambale, Mohamed Salem, Mahmoud Hamed, Selain K. Kasereka, Kyandoghere Kyamakya
publish: 2025 5th International Multidisciplinary Information Technology and Engineering Conference (IMITEC)
abstract: Public bus operators prohibit eating to maintain cleanliness and passenger comfort, yet enforcement still relies on driver observation, which introduces distraction and safety risks. This paper presents a computer vision-based system for detecting eating activities in public buses, designed for real-time operation on edge devices. A hybrid dataset was constructed by combining 958 frames from a public activity recognition source with 650 bus-specific synthetic images generated under diverse lighting, seasonal, and passenger conditions. The data were annotated into five classes (hand, mouth, utensil, food, eat) to capture eating-related actions. A YOLOv8n detector was trained and integrated with a lightweight rule-based inference module to classify each scene as eating, has food but not eating, or no food. Experimental results show that the system achieves an overall mAP50 of 0.714 on movie clips, 0.595 on generated bus data, and 0.677 on the hybrid dataset. Larger features such as hands and mouths are detected reliably, while smaller objects like utensils remain challenging. The findings demonstrate the feasibility of detecting eating in dynamic bus environments and highlight the benefits of hybrid datasets for generalization. This work contributes towards automated enforcement of no-eating regulations in public transportation.

title: Triboelectric Nanogenerators from Incense Stick Ash Waste for Flexible Electronics
authors: Monika Gadhewal, Ritesh Kumar Singh, Shree Prakash Tiwari
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Emerging Electronics (ICEE)
abstract: Growing demand for sustainable energy sources has led to the exploration of novel materials for energy harvesting systems. In this work, triboelectric nanogenerator (TENG) devices were fabricated using incense stick ash (ISA) waste as a tribo-positive material. A readily available and biodegradable ISA by-product, which is primarily composed of carbonaceous content that exhibits excellent surface roughness and dielectric properties favorable for enhancing triboelectric performance. The fabricated ISA-TENG devices exhibit stable and repeatable electrical output under mechanical input with a peak open-circuit voltage of ~36 V and a short-circuit current of ~3.6 µA. These devices demonstrate dual functionality by acting as self-powered sensors when integrated into various body parts and effectively monitoring a range of human physiological activities, including bending, tapping, and joint movements. This work underscores the transformative potential of waste valorization in creating transient electronic systems. By leveraging low-cost and abundant waste material, the ISA-TENGs offer flexible, self-powered health monitoring and sustainable energy harvesting applications.

title: A 7.15-to-7.95GHz Magnetically Enhanced Feedforward Waveform-Shaping CMOS Oscillator with Implicit Common-Mode Noise Cancellation Achieving −146.72 dBc/Hz PN@1MHz and 190.6dBc/Hz FoM
authors: Rui Ma, Wei Deng, Haikun Jia, Juntao Lan, Zhihua Wang, Baoyong Chi
abstract: Pure microwave sources are vital for precision instrumentation, but conventional schemes are bulky and costly. CMOS oscillators are inexpensive and compact yet limited by phase-noise performance. To address this, we present a magnetically enhanced, feedforward waveform-shaping oscillator with an implicit common-mode noise-cancellation technique. Measurements show <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$-146.72 \text{dBc} / \text{Hz} \text{PN}$</tex> at a 1 MHz offset and a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$190.6 \text{dBc} / \text{Hz} \text{FoM}$</tex> at a 7.5 GHz carrier.

title: Adaptive Threshold ISI Encoding for Noise-Resilient SNNs on Memristors Crossbar Arrays
authors: Ishan Jha, Aryan Mishra, Ananda Y. R.
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Emerging Electronics (ICEE)
abstract: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) hold promise for ultra-low-power, event-driven computation, but suffer performance degradation under noisy inputs and device variability. We propose an adaptive threshold-enhanced inter-spike interval (ISI) encoding method that dynamically modulates each neuron’s firing threshold based on its instantaneous input current, stabilizing temporal codes against noise. This encoding is integrated into a two-terminal memristor crossbar-based computing-in-memory (CIM) architecture, exploiting parallel analog dot-product operations to minimize data movement and energy. On the MNIST benchmark with additive Gaussian noise 10%, our adaptive threshold SNN achieves 88% accuracy, 10% higher than a fixed threshold baseline. This approach enables robust, energy-efficient neuromorphic hardware implementations.

title: Reconstructing Protected Biometric Templates from Binary Authentication Results
authors: Eliron Rahimi, Margarita Osadchy, Orr Dunkelman
publish: 2025 IEEE International Joint Conference on Biometrics (IJCB)
abstract: Biometric data is considered to be very private and highly sensitive. As such, many methods for biometric template protection were considered over the years — from biohashing and specially crafted feature extraction procedures, to the use of cryptographic solutions such as Fuzzy Commitments or the use of Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE).A key question that arises is how much protection these solutions can offer when the adversary can inject samples, and observe the outputs of the system. While for systems that return the similarity score, one can use attacks such as hill-climbing, for systems where the adversary can only learn whether the authentication attempt was successful, this question remained open.In this paper, we show that it is indeed possible to reconstruct the biometric template by just observing the success/failure of the authentication attempt (given the ability to inject a sufficient amount of faces). Our attack achieves negligible template reconstruction loss and enables full recovery of facial images through a generative inversion method, forming a pipeline from binary scores to high-resolution facial images that successfully pass the system more than 98% of the time. Our results are, of course, independent of the protection mechanism used by the system.

title: Design and development of MEMS-based cantilever beam for the low-frequency energy harvester
authors: Pramod Gaikwad, Boby George, Nandita DasGupta, Amitava DasGupta
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Emerging Electronics (ICEE)
abstract: Silicon cantilevers with integrated proof mass and deposited metal/piezo/metal stack is designed, simulated, fabricated and characterized for energy harvesting from low frequency (< 300 Hz) ambient vibrations. The cantilevers with resonant frequencies (f<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf>) < 300 Hz are fabricated by using MEMS technology using SOI (Silicon-on-Insulator) wafers, which offer precise control over the thicknesses of the beam and the proof mass. Lead zirconate titanate (PZT) and Aluminium nitride (AlN) are chosen as the piezoelectric materials for our study. While PZT has a very high piezoelectric coefficient, AlN has the advantages of low dielectric constant and CMOS process compatibility. In this work, first, a parametric study is carried out by varying the various geometrical parameters of the structure to obtain the required f<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf> through simulations using COMSOL Multiphysics. Simulations are also carried out to obtain the output voltage and power that can be obtained from these structures to ensure that they are suitable for energy harvesting. Design iterations were carried out through feedback from fabrication runs to arrive at the optimum structure. The f<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf> of the structures fabricated with AlN as a piezo material was measured using the Laser Doppler Vibrometer (LDV) and a good match was obtained with simulation results. Different structures with f<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">r</inf> varying from 70 Hz to 300 Hz were identified. Simulations indicate that 2 mm long, 100 µm wide and 15 µm thick beams supporting a 2 mm × 2 mm × 0.5 mm proof mass is capable of providing more than 45 µW of power at an output voltage of nearly 5 V with PZT and 25 µW of power with AlN at the output voltage of more than 15 V under 1 g vibration conditions.

title: Equivalent Modeling Method for PMSG Wind Farms Considering Negative-Sequence Control Strategies
authors: Li Wang, Yue Xiang, Cheng Jiang, Peng Ai
publish: 2025 6th International Symposium on New Energy and Electrical Technology (ISNEET)
abstract: With the increasing penetration of renewable energy, equivalent modeling of renewable energy power plants has become essential. First, this paper analyzes two common negative-sequence control strategies to identify all possible response characteristics of wind turbines. Then, the classification boundaries between different response characteristics are derived based on their operating principles. Finally, a single-machine equivalent modeling method for each classified wind turbine cluster is proposed. The response characteristics of individual wind turbines are analyzed based on the CloudPSS simulation platform, while the proposed equivalent modeling method is validated in the IEEE 39-node system.

title: Stator Grounding Protection of Large Synchronous Turbo-generator Considering Intermittent Grounding Fault
authors: Haiying Zhang, Juntao Li, Danfeng Wang, Xiaojian Wang
abstract: Stator grounding protection is essential for the safe operation of large synchronous turbo-generators. However, conventional grounding protection method struggles to reliably clear intermittent grounding faults. This difficulty arises because such faults temporarily disappear during the protection relay's delay period, preventing timely operation. Therefore, based on the analysis of an actual intermittent grounding fault in a large synchronous turbo-generator, this paper presents an improved stator grounding protection method utilizing incremental accumulation algorithm. Simulation results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, offering valuable insights for addressing similar generator protection challenges.

title: Keynotes
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Trust, Privacy and Security in Intelligent Systems, and Applications (TPS-ISA)

title: Distributed Consensus Network: A Modularized Communication Framework and Reliability Probabilistic Analysis_supp1-3667834.pdf
authors: Lei Zhang
abstract: In this paper, we propose a modularized framework for communication processes applicable to crash and Byzantine fault-tolerant consensus protocols. We abstract basic communication components and show that the communication process of the classic consensus protocols such as RAFT, single-decree Paxos, PBFT, and Hotstuff, can be represented by the combination of communication components. Based on the proposed framework, we develop an approach to analyze the consensus reliability of different protocols, where link loss and node failure are measured as a probability. We propose two latency optimization methods and implement a RAFT system to verify our theoretical analysis and the effectiveness of the proposed latency optimization methods. We also discuss decreasing consensus failure rate by adjusting protocol designs. This paper provides theoretical guidance for the design of future consensus systems with a low consensus failure rate and latency under the possible communication loss.

title: A Privacy-Fidelity Tradeoff Framework in Post-Processed Machine Learning
authors: Md Faisal Ahmed, Zhengdao Wang
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Trust, Privacy and Security in Intelligent Systems, and Applications (TPS-ISA)
abstract: Membership inference attacks (MIA) pose significant threats to the privacy of machine learning models by allowing adversaries to determine whether a given data point was used in training. In this paper, we present a principled theoretical framework to analyze the tradeoff between privacy and fidelity in model predictions. We adopt an information-theoretic perspective, modeling membership leakage as mutual information between the model's prediction features (e.g., confidence, loss, entropy) and the membership indicator. Fidelity is defined as the distortion between the original prediction distribution and its transformed counterpart, measured using divergence metrics. Our analysis reveals inherent limits in the privacy-fidelity space and provides a foundation for evaluating or designing output transformation techniques that mitigate information leakage. This work offers a unified lens to reason about privacy risks in machine learning inference pipelines and informs the development of practical defenses in future work.

title: Research on the "Electricity-Carbon" Synergistic Optimization Method of the "Source-Grid-Load-Storage" Integrated System at the Park Level
authors: Yun Xu, Yuying Shao, Jiayu Yang, Guodong Zhang, Zheng Li, Bingjie Wu, Xiangzhou Chen, Xianxian Chen
publish: 2025 6th International Symposium on New Energy and Electrical Technology (ISNEET)
abstract: The park-level "source-grid-load-storage" integrated system is an important carrier for achieving low-carbon energy transformation. Conducting research on "electricity-carbon" synergistic optimization is of great practical significance for advancing the "dual carbon" goals. This paper constructs a system model including photovoltaic, wind power, gas turbine, energy storage and adjustable load, and establishes a multi-objective optimization function with the goals of minimizing operating costs and carbon emissions, taking into account multiple constraints such as power balance, equipment operation, grid safety and carbon emissions. The optimization problem is solved by combining the weighted sum method with the ε -constraint method, and three typical scenarios are designed for comparative analysis. The results show that the electricity-carbon synergy optimization scheme can achieve a 17.5% reduction in carbon emissions with only a 5.3% increase in operating costs, effectively balancing economic and low-carbon demands and providing theoretical basis and methodological support for the optimal operation of the park's energy system.

title: Reliability Improvement Strategy of Active Distribution Networks Based on Proactive Load Transfer
authors: Zhipeng Huang, Fadong Peng, Mingjie Zhang, Yangjue Huang, Yuanjun Chen
publish: 2025 6th International Symposium on New Energy and Electrical Technology (ISNEET)
abstract: To address the issue of distribution network power supply reliability affected by large-scale distributed photovoltaic integration, this paper proposes a reliability improvement strategy for PV-integrated distribution networks based on proactive load transfer, considering the typical wiring structure of distribution networks. Taking the maximum line power supply reliability within a time period as the objective function, the method establishes an operation control model for proactive load transfer and solves the model through formula expansion. Taking the looped line group of an actual distribution network as an example, a simulation comparison is conducted between the traditional control method and the proposed method. The results verify the effectiveness of the proposed method and demonstrate its superiority in improving power supply reliability.

title: Generative Diffusion Modelling for High Quality Wafer Image Data Augmentation, Defect Detection and Image Annotation
authors: Ravi Kumar Balija
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Emerging Electronics (ICEE)
abstract: Modern semiconductor manufacturing processes are inherently complex and require precise coordination to achieve the desired outcomes. Thousands of tools and workflows must operate in harmony across various manufacturing sectors, teams, and fabrication facilities, often spanning multiple geographic locations. This orchestration is critical for meeting business objectives, including attaining high yield rates and maximizing revenue. Additionally, effective coordination of personnel is essential to ensure operational efficiency. In Semiconductor manufacturing, achieving high yield rates is paramount for meeting year-over-year revenue targets and adapting to volatile market demands. Reducing wafer defect rates during the manufacturing process is a crucial factor in this endeavor. However, many organizations lack sufficient collections of wafer images necessary for comprehensive defect analysis and mitigation. To address the challenges, the paper presents the application of advanced deep learning techniques, specifically generative diffusion modeling. We implement diffusion models for image data augmentation, defect detection, and annotation, offering a robust solution for organizations aiming to improve wafer quality and yield through enhanced data-driven methodologies.

title: Advances in Agricultural Automation in South Africa: Technologies, Applications, and Future Research Directions
authors: Rotimi-Williams Bello, Roseline Oluwaseun Ogundokun, Pius A. Owolawi, Etienne A. van Wyk, Chunling Tu, Celestine Uche Agwi
publish: 2025 5th International Multidisciplinary Information Technology and Engineering Conference (IMITEC)
abstract: Agricultural automation is an emerging transformative solution to the challenges facing global food security, labor shortages, and the demand for sustainability in farming practices. In this paper, a concise but comprehensive overview is provided of the key automation technologies in agriculture, including Artificial Intelligence (AI), Computer Vision, Robotics, and Internet of Things (IoT) systems. A review is conducted on recent advances in precision agriculture, autonomous machinery, and automated post-harvest processing. A narrative review approach with systematic elements is adopted in this study for synthesis rigorousness, transparency, and reproducibility of existing works on agricultural automation in South Africa. Four key stages made up the methodology, namely literature identification, screening and selection, thematic coding, and synthesis. Selected case studies in South Africa were compared with benchmarks from countries with developed economies and those with emerging contexts for identification of gaps, opportunities, and scalability potential. Several real-world case studies were highlighted and the barriers to adoption were examined, including cost, infrastructure, and farmer readiness. Finally, the paper outlines sustainable future research directions for advancing farming practices through integration of smart technologies, whereby climate-resilient and high-yield agricultural systems can be achieved. This review produces unique insights by proposing a conceptual framework (builds on three pillars) for adopting agricultural automation across Africa.

title: 21.9 A -82.3dB THD+N 60V Fully Integrated Shunt-Resistor-Based In-Line Current Sensor with DLL-Assisted Dynamic Body-Biasing Technique
authors: Heng Ma, Huajun Zhang, Qinwen Fan
abstract: Floating-<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{m}}$</tex>-based current sensors are effective in rejecting PWM common-mode voltage. But their linearity drops at high voltages due to substrate-current-induced body effect in LDMOS devices. To address this issue, this work introduces a DLL-assisted dynamic body biasing sensor in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.18 \mu \mathrm{m}$</tex> BCD, improving HD2 by 31 dB and THD+N by 14 dB. And a peak THD+N of −82.3 dB is achieved in the meanwhile. It supports 60 V common-mode, rejects PWM up to 2 MHz, and enables high-linearity sensing for high power audio and motor-driver systems.

title: DArFace: Deformation Aware Robustness for Low Quality Face Recognition
authors: Sadaf Gulshad, Abdullah Aldahlawi Thakaa
publish: 2025 IEEE International Joint Conference on Biometrics (IJCB)
abstract: Facial recognition systems have achieved remarkable success by leveraging deep neural networks, advanced loss functions, and large-scale datasets. However, their performance often deteriorates in real-world scenarios involving low-quality facial images. Such degradations, common in surveillance footage or standoff imaging include low resolution, motion blur, and various distortions, resulting in a substantial domain gap from the high-quality data typically used during training. While existing approaches attempt to address robustness by modifying network architectures or modeling global spatial transformations, they frequently overlook local, non-rigid deformations that are inherently present in real-world settings. In this work, we introduce DArFace, a Deformation-Aware robust Face recognition framework that enhances robustness to such degradations without requiring paired high- and low-quality training samples. Our method adversarially integrates both global transformations (e.g., rotation, translation) and local elastic deformations during training to simulate realistic low-quality conditions. Moreover, we introduce a contrastive objective to enforce identity consistency across different deformed views. Extensive evaluations on low-quality benchmarks including TinyFace, IJB-B, and IJB-C demonstrate that DArFace surpasses state-of-the-art methods, with significant gains attributed to the inclusion of local deformation modeling.

title: From Features to Embeddings: Extending the Temporal-Persistence Principle to Deep-Learning Eye-movement Biometric
authors: Mehedi Hasan Raju, Lee Friedman, Dillon Lohr, Oleg V. Komogortsev
publish: 2025 IEEE International Joint Conference on Biometrics (IJCB)
