title: Proactive Power Management-Based Supply Regulation with Online Learning for Variation-Tolerant Workload-Aware Droop Mitigation in 28nm CMOS
authors: Xi Chen, Andrew Liss, William Covington, Qiankai Cao, Yiqi Li, Kang Wei, Raveesh Magod, Muhammad Khellah, Xin Zhang, Jie Gu
abstract: A 28 nm SoC solution with integrated proactive power management for droop mitigation is demonstrated combining a neural droop management unit, integrated high speed power converter, and an online learning engine to combat the PDN and workload variations. The 28nm test chip integrated with CPU and accelerators achieves 59% worst-case droop reduction, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$48 \times$</tex> throttling reduction, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>91 {\%}$</tex> regulator peak efficiency, reducing performance degradation from prior fixed-model or throttling-only schemes.

title: Residential Building Energy Performance Analysis Using Machine Learning Algorithms
authors: Sannidhi D Math, Nilay D Trivedi, Aditya Thapa, B R K Holla, Nikhil Pachauri
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Network Systems (CINS)
abstract: The HVAC system can achieve low energy consumption as ML-based models accurately estimate the building’s energy use and load demands. Therefore, in this work, an extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost) ensemble model is proposed for predicting energy usage based on heating and cooling Loads (HL and CL). Furthermore, RF, LR, KNN, and SVR are also designed for comparison analysis. The results show that XGBoost outperforms all the applied algorithms, achieving the lowest values of RMSE (0.407 and 0.858) and MSE (0.166 and 0.737) in both cases. Furthermore, its performance is also compared with the models presented in the literature. Finally, it can be concluded that the proposed XGBoost is superior, robust, and efficient for predicting HL and CL, respectively.

title: 29.6 A 65nm CMOS Hydrogel-Based Dual Fluorescence Sensor for Bioavailable Phosphorus Detection
authors: Ting-Yu Cheng, Raj S. Mukkamala, Reinaldo E. Alcalde, Mohamadamin Panahandeh, Akshit Agarwal, Julia A. Kornfield, Dianne K. Newman, Azita Emami
abstract: A hydrogel-based single-culture dual-fluorescence sensor is presented for bioavailable phosphorus (P) detection. The sensor achieves 6.6fA sensitivity with a 15.5 dB SNR and an output noise of 1.97 mV at 182 ms integration time. A large dynamic range is enabled through high-precision automatic background calibration with a coarse and fine control loop. The sensor was validated using hydrogels embedded with a bioreporter strain and successfully detected both P limitation and cell viability.

title:
13.4 An Inverse-Designed Passively Coupled N-Path Filter with g
                    <sub>m</sub>
                    -Boosted Active HBT Switches
authors: Vinay Chenna, Hossein Hashemi
abstract: A <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.8-\text{to}-2.6 \text{GHz} \mathrm{N}$</tex>-path filter with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{g}_{\mathrm{m}}$</tex>-boosted HBT switches and inverse-designed nonresonant passive networks is presented to enhance dynamic range and tunability of passively coupled higher-order N-path filters. A hybrid real-binary inverse design algorithm capable of inserting variable-size MIM capacitors in layouts enables synthesis of compact multilayered low-GHz passives. A BiCMOS filter prototype achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>15 \text{dBm}$</tex> P1dB, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<5 ~\text{dB}$</tex> noise figure and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$<4 ~\text{dB}$</tex> insertion loss across the full tuning range.

title:
A 0.008mm
                    <sup>2</sup>
                    16-to-1600 MHz All-Digital Fractional Divider Using AUX-DLL for Background LMS-Based DTC Calibration
authors: Ahmed Elkholy, Yousr Ismail, Zhi Huang, Adesh Garg, Ali Nazemi, Jun Cao, Afshin Momtaz
abstract: An all-digital high-performance standalone fractional divider (FDIV) is presented. It leverages a robust replica-free least-mean square (LMS)-based digital-to-time converter (DTC) background calibration using a compact auxiliary delay locked loop (AUX-DLL). The proposed FDIV provides a wide output frequency range <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(16-1600 \text{MHz})$</tex> with very fine frequency resolution (50b) and achieves worst-case integrated jitter performance of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$350 \text{fs}_{\text{rms}}$</tex>, while occupying only <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.008 \text{mm}^{2}$</tex>.

title: Handheld Portable Fluorescence based LFA Reader: Design and Development for Broad Applications
authors: J Gireesh Kumar, Roshan Makkar, Rohit Srivastava
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Emerging Electronics (ICEE)
abstract: Fluorescent based lateral flow assay (LFA) strips play a crucial role in the point of care qualitative and quantitative diagnostics. They are widely used in healthcare and many other fields due to their low cost, easy to use and rapid diagnosis nature. However, as their usage is expanding globally, the necessity for improving the sensitivity and specificity without compromising their advantages is of significant demand. This is being achieved by the usage of the LFA readers by quantifying the results. In the present work, a LFA reader was developed to measure the concentrations of mercaptopropionic acid capped cadmium telluride quantum dots (MPA-CdTe QDs) in the range of 0.1 to 1.4 mg/ml. The device measurements were highly correlated with the concentrations with a R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> value > 0.93.

title: Deep Spectro-Temporal Embeddings from the M5 CNN with Spectral Clustering for Unsupervised Heartbeat Sound Clustering
authors: Tinashe Crispen Garidzira, Caroline Gurajena
publish: 2025 5th International Multidisciplinary Information Technology and Engineering Conference (IMITEC)
abstract: Analysis heartbeat sounds are crucial for spotting cardiovascular problems early, particularly in healthcare settings with limited resources. This study proposes the use of supervised classification and unsupervised clustering of heartbeat sounds using deep Spectro-temporal features extracted from the M5 Convolutional Neural Network architecture. In this work, dataset obtained from Kaggle was used. The classification component achieved perfect performance on the test set, with a loss of 0.048 and classification accuracy of 100%, confirming the model's robustness in distinguishing between heartbeat categories. For unsupervised clustering, Spectral Clustering was applied to the M5 embeddings, resulting in a Silhouette Score of 0.303, Calinski-Harabasz Index of 63.580, and Davies-Bouldin Index of 1.020. These metrics indicate moderately distinct clusters reflective of the underlying heartbeat sound types. This research demonstrates the effectiveness of combining deep CNN-based feature extraction with spectral methods for both classification and discovery of structure in medical audio data. The proposed solution offers a scalable, label-efficient approach for supporting diagnostic decision-making in cardiac care.

title: Smart Pesticide Spraying System Using AIoT
authors: Jeyakumar P, Selvakumar George, Amutha A, Sree Sudharshana R, Srinithi S, Subasri P
publish: 2025 First International Conference of Advances in Engineering and Computing Technologies for Sustainable Development (AECTSD)
abstract: In the era of precision agriculture, effective pest management plays a critical role in enhancing crop yield and minimizing pesticide overuse. This work introduces a Smart Pesticide Spraying System integrated with a Remote-Controlled Car to optimize pesticide application and reduce environmental impact. The system features an Artificial Intelligence (AI)-enabled camera for real-time pest detection, ultrasonic sensors for obstacle detection, and ultrasonic nozzles for precise pesticide spraying. Controlled wirelessly via a remote, the vehicle allows users to navigate fields and spray pesticides based on pest infestation levels. The pesticide tank, spray nozzles, and control circuits are mounted on a robust chassis powered by a LithiumIon battery, with an optional solar panel for energy efficiency. This proposed smart spraying solution enhances pesticide efficiency, reduces human exposure to harmful chemicals, and promotes eco-friendly agricultural practices. Future upgrades could include adding Wi-Fi connectivity, integrating machine learning for pest prediction, and developing a mobile app interface for remote operation.

title: Effect of Traps During Off-state Degradation in AlGaN/GaN HEMTs
authors: Shani Tiwari, Jaya Jha
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Emerging Electronics (ICEE)
abstract: AlGaN/GaN High Electron Mobility Transistors (HEMTs) are widely used in RF and power applications but face long-term reliability issues under high voltages, leading to performance degradation. This study investigates the off-state degradation in Al<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>/AlGaN/GaN HEMTs, especially the effects of carrier trapping at the Al<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>/AlGaN interface on the transfer characteristics. Measurements taken after applying an off-state stress voltage of -10V at the gate and 20V at the drain reveal degradation in the transfer curve, indicating electron trapping at the Al2O3/AlGaN interface. Using calibrated two-dimensional TCAD simulations, we have tried to replicate pre- and post-stress behaviors of different device structures, decoupling electron and hole trapping mechanisms to some extent.

title: MorphoBlock: A Learnable Morphological Dilation Module for Efficient and Accurate Deep Vision Models
authors: Giri D, Larson Albin L, Michael Nirmal G, Arun Prakash S, B. Arun Kumar
publish: 2025 First International Conference of Advances in Engineering and Computing Technologies for Sustainable Development (AECTSD)
abstract: A persistent tradeoff exists between model performance and computational resources in deep learning for computer vision, and while recent models have demonstrated high performance, they are limited to being deployed to resource constrained edge devices due to their size and memory requirements [3] [18]. In response to this limitation, we present the Morphological Dilation Block (MorphoBlock) – a novel, lightweight, fully differentiable neural module that utilizes mathematical morphology to enhance deep feature maps via adaptive dilation [4] [15]. The MorphoBlock provides the ability to dynamically expand salient regions within the input feature map and allows for the aggregation of important spatial patterns through a learnable sampling operation, which are both learned in a data driven manner [5]. We evaluate the efficacy and generality of the MorphoBlock by embedding it as a plug-and-play module into five state-of-the-art architectures: ConvNeXt-Tiny, Efficient ViT, Mobile ViT, Efficient Former, and EdgeNeXt [7], and show consistent improvements in performance for each of these architectures on a challenging fine-grained image classification dataset [8]. Most importantly, we find that the addition of the MorphoBlock to the ConvNeXt-Tiny architecture (referred to as ConvNeXt-Tiny+) results in a 18.5% increase in the top-1 test accuracy, resulting in a test accuracy of 96.8% [9] at an additional cost of approximately +0.02% model parameters [9]. Therefore, a single enhanced model achieves a new state-of-the-art result on the efficiency frontier and performs better than the baseline model and multi-model fusion ensemble models with respect to accuracy, parameter efficiency, power consumption, and training time [10].

title: Uplift Characteristics of Jumpers on Tension Towers under Slope-Induced Upwind
authors: Linming Jin, Xianqiang Li, Shunji Zhang, Hao Wu, Bin Lan, Ziling You
publish: 2025 6th International Symposium on New Energy and Electrical Technology (ISNEET)
abstract: Under the action of slope-induced upwind, tension tower jumpers are prone to uplift, and the electrical clearance may approach the critical discharge distance of adjacent conductors or fittings, potentially causing insulation failure. This study employs finite element simulations to investigate the uplift behavior of jumpers under slope-induced upwind and systematically analyzes the effects of wind speed and wind direction. The results indicate that, under various wind speeds and wind direction angles, the maximum uplift consistently occurs near the position corresponding to a 6 m arc length. As wind speed increases, the uplift displacement increases significantly with an accelerating growth trend, reaching up to 0.4337 m at a wind speed of 30 m/s. Conversely, the uplift displacement decreases with increasing wind direction angle, with the maximum uplift occurring at 0°, reaching approximately 0.22 m. This study provides technical support for evaluating the uplift-induced discharge risk of jumper cables on tension towers, and serves as a basis for future assessment of flashover risk under various wind speed and wind direction conditions.

title: Explainable But Vulnerable: Adversarial Attacks on XAI Explanation in Cybersecurity Applications
authors: Maraz Mia, Mir Mehedi A. Pritom
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Trust, Privacy and Security in Intelligent Systems, and Applications (TPS-ISA)
abstract: Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) has aided machine learning (ML) researchers with the ability to scrutinize the decisions of the black-box models. XAI methods enable looking deep inside the models' behavior, eventually generating explanations along with a perceived trust and transparency. However, the degree of trustworthiness varies across XAI methods. It is evident that XAI methods can themselves be a victim of post-hoc adversarial attacks that manipulate the expected outcome from the explanation module. Among such attack tactics, fairwashing explanation (FE), manipulation explanation (ME), and backdoor-enabled manipulation attacks (BD) are the notable ones. In this paper, we try to understand these adversarial attack techniques, tactics, and procedures (TTPs) on explanation alteration and thus the effect on the model's decisions. We have explored six distinct attack procedures on post-hoc explanation methods such as SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanation), and IG (Integrated Gradients), and investigated those adversarial attacks in cybersecurity applications scenarios such as phishing, malware, intrusion, and fraudulent website detection. Our experimental study reveals the actual effectiveness of these attacks, thus providing an urgency for immediate attention to enhance the resiliency of XAI methods and their applications.

title: Optimal Capacity Configuration of Electrochemical Energy Storage Systems Based on Factor Analysis
authors: Chengwen Zhou, Bing Ouyang, Qingsen Cai, Kaiyan Wang, Li Ma, Jiandong Jin, Zhangwu Yang
abstract: The integration of electrochemical energy storage into regional power grids has diverse and complex effects on the operation of the power grid. When configuring capacity, optimizing only a single indicator cannot guarantee the optimal comprehensive performance of the power grid. In view of this, this article establishes a comprehensive evaluation index system for the integration of electrochemical energy storage into the power grid, and each evaluation index has inherent correlation. Factor analysis can analyze the intrinsic connections between various evaluation indicators, extract the main common factors to describe the comprehensive indicators, and explain the intrinsic connections between common factors and various indicators, which can reduce the difficulty of model solving. Therefore, this article adopts factor analysis to optimize the configuration of electrochemical energy storage systems in regional power grids, evaluate the comprehensive evaluation indicators under different configuration capacities, and plan the optimal capacity of electrochemical energy storage systems.

title: Stackelberg Game-Based Optimal Operation of a Shared Energy Storage–Integrated Energy Microgrid with Electric Vehicle Aggregation
authors: Boyu Wang, Zhiyuan Wang, Hao Zhao, Hongyan Ma
abstract: The progressive electrification of buildings and transport increases the operational complexity of distribution-level energy systems. This paper models the day-ahead operation of a commercial electricity–heat microgrid with a shared energy storage system (ESS) and an aggregated fleet of bidirectionally connected electric vehicles (EVs) as a bi-level Stackelberg game between a microgrid operator and a user aggregator. Given time-varying electricity and heat prices, the follower minimises operating cost by jointly scheduling flexible building loads, shared ESS power and EV charging/discharging under comfort and mobility constraints; for a fixed price trajectory the follower problem is convex. At the upper level, a differential evolution metaheuristic searches over price trajectories to approximate the Stackelberg equilibrium. Numerical results for a 24 h case study show that the proposed coordination scheme narrows the net-load peak–valley gap, improves renewable utilisation and increases economic benefits for both the operator and users relative to uncoordinated operation and a single-level benchmark.

title: Ethical AI and UX Design in Employee Monitoring Systems: A Framework for Balancing Privacy and Productivity
authors: Mohammed Misbahul Khair, Jaidev Jayakumar, Sachin Kumar Gupta
publish: 2025 First International Conference of Advances in Engineering and Computing Technologies for Sustainable Development (AECTSD)
abstract: Monitoring technologies of employees have developed very quickly due to the combination of artificial intelligence, although the issue concerning privacy, fairness, transparency, and worker autonomy are still a major obstacle to using them ethically. In this paper, a comprehensive Ethical AI, UX-based architecture is introduced, which rests on the idea of privacy-preserving computation, fairness-conscience modelling, human-centric explanation design, and organised human-in-the-loop workflow to reinvent the concept of monitoring systems. The differential privacy, bias-mitigation algorithms and interpretable machine learning models were used to analyse multimodal data in the form of behavioural logs, activity traces and contextual metadata. The system also implements a layered user experience (UX) design to facilitate clear disclosures, informative visualisation and low cognitive-load decision interfaces among employees and managers. An experimental analysis conducted has proven that the suggested framework enhances predictive reliability with a high level of ethical protection. The model had a 611% improvement in one of the main metrics of key accuracy, and the false positives dropped by 35 percent, and the indicators of fairness went up more than 70 percent over a traditional benchmark. According to the user studies, the explainability-oriented UX design works to cause more trust and improve an understanding of the automated decisions and reduced cognitive load. These findings prove that ethical principles do not have to undermine performance instead, they improve system resilience and societal acceptability.

title: Channel Encapsulation and Annealing Optimization for Robust Bottom-Gate ITO FETs
authors: Aakash Deshpande, Nihal Raut, Veeresh Deshpande
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Emerging Electronics (ICEE)
abstract: Amorphous oxide semiconductor (AOS) FETs face reliability challenges such as bias temperature instability (BTI), subthreshold swing degradation, and flat-band voltage shifts, originating from defects in the channel, gate oxide, and their interfaces. These challenges limit their use in embedded memory applications such as 2T embedded DRAM, where long retention and low leakage are essential. In this work, we demonstrate channel encapsulation as an effective strategy to mitigate these issues, achieving up to 1.5× improved immunity against both positive and negative bias stress compared to uncapped devices, while preserving threshold voltage, ON current, and subthreshold swing. These results establish channel encapsulation as a promising pathway toward reliable AOS FET as well as enable optimized top gate integration in BEOL-compatible 3D embedded memory technologies.

title: Robust High-Frequency Wirebonding of a Power Amplifier to a Wideband D-Band Antenna Array
authors: Samuel Rimbaut, Reinier Broucke, Kamil Yavuz Kapusuz, Nishant Singh, Guy Torfs, Sam Lemey, Hendrik Rogier
publish: 2025 IEEE Electrical Design of Advanced Packaging and Systems (EDAPS)
abstract: A tailored wirebonding process is proposed, conceived to minimize interconnect losses between active components and printed-circuit-board (PCB)-integrated structures. This is achieved by thinning the integrated circuit (IC) and embedding it into a milled cavity in the PCB carrier to level the IC and PCB surfaces, thereby minimizing bond wire length. In this way, a silicon-germanium BiCMOS D-band power amplifier is compactly integrated on the backside of a PCB-based <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4 \times 1$</tex> full D-band antenna array with only 1 dB of insertion loss. The effects of variations within expected tolerances in the different processing steps, being the thinning, milling and bondwiring are analyzed to ascertain the robustness of the proposed approach. The thinning and milling step is found to be the most crucial one, with the maximum considered deviations leading to a gain decrease by 2.5 dB. If this step is well controlled, we find that a decrease by 1.5 dB is expected, which agrees well with the measured decrease by under 2 dB.

title: Diminution of commensurate fractional-order systems based on frequency responses optimization
authors: Shibendu Mahata, Ayush Paul, Abhirup Das, Ashutosh Chatterjee, Priyanshu Raj, Akhilesh Gupta, Praveen Kumar Mondal
publish: 2025 First International Conference of Advances in Engineering and Computing Technologies for Sustainable Development (AECTSD)
abstract: A frequency-domain based minimization problem is formulated for order diminution of commensurate fractional-order systems in this paper. The applicability of the snake optimizer (SO) is demonstrated to solve the proposed problem. Simulations are extensively conducted to study the accuracy, computational efficiency, and convergence of SO. Two examples are considered to highlight the effectiveness of the proposed approach as compared to the reported literature. The ||H<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">∞</inf>|| metric achieved for the proposed ROMs for the considered examples are 0.1207 and 0.0043, respectively, which is significantly smaller as compared to those yielded by the most competitive method published in the literature (0.1426 and 0.0497, respectively).

title: Evaluation and Optimization of off-Grid and On-Grid Photovoltaic Power Systems for Household Electrification in South Africa
authors: Lenardo Pillay, Yokwana Xolani, Mbuyu Sumbwanyambe
publish: 2025 5th International Multidisciplinary Information Technology and Engineering Conference (IMITEC)
abstract: South Africa's persistent electricity shortages, rising tariffs, and unreliable grid highlight the need for alternative household energy solutions. This paper evaluates and optimizes on-grid and off-grid photovoltaic (PV) systems for a typical middle-class household in Johannesburg using quantitative modelling. Household load data were aggregated at 30-minute and hourly intervals and simulated with HOMER Energy and HelioScope, supported by Microsoft Excel analysis. System design followed SANS 10142 and NRS 097-2-2 standards, incorporating inverter and battery constraints. The optimization objective was to minimize lifecycle cost while maximizing energy autonomy and reliability under local solar conditions. Economic assessment considered Net Present Cost (NPC), Levelized Cost of Electricity (LCOE), Internal Rate of Return (IRR), and payback period, alongside environmental performance. Results indicate that the on-grid PV system achieved an NPC of R35 985 and an LCOE of R0.353/kWh, meeting 89.7 % of household demand and exporting surplus electricity to the grid. The off-grid system enabled energy independence but required higher capital investment due to storage, with an NPC of R R10 9357.50 and LCOE of R4.71/kWh. The ongrid and off-grid PV systems significantly reduced environmental impacts, lowering annual CO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf> emissions by 3 135 kg and 8859 kg respectively. The study concludes that on-grid PV offers greater cost efficiency for urban households, while off-grid PV enhances resilience and is more suitable for rural areas where transmission costs are high. Findings provide insights for engineers, policymakers, and homeowners seeking sustainable electrification pathways.

title: Switch Transformer-Based Reinforcement Learning Method for Power Supply Induced Jitter (PSIJ) Reduction in High Bandwidth Memory (HBM)
authors: Jaegeun Bae, Byeongmok Kim, Keeyoung Son, Taein Shin, Haeseok Suh, Jiwon Yoon, Keunwoo Kim, Youngsu Yoon, Junho Park, Hyunseo Uhm, Eunji Seo, Inyoung Choi, Joungho Kim
publish: 2025 IEEE Electrical Design of Advanced Packaging and Systems (EDAPS)
abstract: In this paper, we propose a novel power distribution network (PDN) design agent that reduces power supply induced jitter (PSIJ) in high bandwidth memory using a Switch Transformer-based reinforcement learning (RL). The Switch Transformer architecture sparsely activates weights in feed-forward network of a conventional transformer model, allowing computational cost to remain low while scaling model size. As Switch Transformer may suffer from instabilities in training loss, proximal policy optimization method is adopted to maximize training efficiency while ensuring stability. The RL reward includes both PSIJ and amount of decoupling capacitor (decap), enabling the agent to satisfy target PSIJ while using minimal number of decap. For validation, performance of the proposed method is compared against random search, genetic algorithm, and Transformer-based RL, where the proposed method consistently outperformed all comparison methods.

title: 2.1 AMD Instinct MI350 Series GPUs: CDNA 4-Based 3D-Stacked 3nm XCDs and 6nm IODs for AI Applications
authors: Ramasamy Adaikkalavan, Alan Smith, Teja Singh, Sundar Rangarajan, Eric Chapman, Samuel Naffziger, Subramaniam Maiyuran, Candy Chen, Sriram Sundaram, Mark Silla, Duncan Law, Kathy Hoover, Samuel Lipson, Kevin Duda, Vinay Parthasarathy, Deepesh John, Hanish Vemulapalli, Srinivas Pavan Kumar Gade
abstract: The AMD Instinct MI350 Series GPU, based on a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4^{\text {th }}$</tex>-generation AMD CDNA architecture, features eight stacked accelerator complex dies (XCD), two I/O dies (IOD) interconnected with AMD Infinity Fabric, and interfaces for eight stacks of 12-hi HBM3E memory. The MI350 Series GPU delivers over <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 \times$</tex> generational inference performance increase by improving energy efficiency, increasing compute throughput, supporting new lower-precision FP6/FP4 datatypes, and increasing both memory bandwidth and capacity.

title: A Fault Diagnosis Method for Transformer Windings by Integrating FRA Phase Response with SSA-SVM
authors: Junhui Xiong, Yi Zhu, Jinwei Qi, Fan Ye, Xinheng Xiong
abstract: Frequency Response Analysis (FRA) is a crucial offline method for detecting winding deformation in transformers. To fully leverage the phase information of frequency response curves and reveal their intrinsic characteristics, this paper proposes a fault diagnosis model that effectively integrates FRA phase response with a Support Vector Machine (SVM) optimized by the Sparrow Search Algorithm (SSA). First, a self-designed disk-type winding model is utilized to simulate four typical mechanical faults—Axial Displacement, Radial Buckling, Disk Space Variation, and Inter-disk Short Circuit with different severity levels, and the corresponding FRA dataset are acquired. Subsequently, real part, imaginary part, and magnitude vectors are calculated based on magnitude-frequency and phase-frequency characteristics. By analyzing the trends of various numerical indicators with respect to fault severity, a set of potential indicators exhibiting high dispersion and strong monotonicity are selected as feature inputs. Finally, an SSA-optimized SVM classification model is established and compared against SVM models optimized by Particle Swarm Optimization and Grid Search. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed potential indicators significantly improve diagnostic accuracy across different fault types and severity levels compared to using only traditional magnitude-frequency features. Moreover, the SSA-SVM model achieves the highest diagnostic accuracy among all compared models, validating the effectiveness of phase information in feature extraction and the superiority of SSA in optimizing SVM parameters.

title: Wafer-scale graphene Josephson field-effect transistors and device models for superconducting integrated circuits
authors: Shanuka Gamaethige, Markus Lehtisalo, Kari Stadius, Heorhii Bohuslavskyi, Klaara Viisanen, Jussi Ryynänen, Andrey Generalov
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Emerging Electronics (ICEE)
abstract: The superconducting transistor, or Josephson Field Effect Transistor (JoFET), is a versatile building block for ultra-low-power and high-energy-efficiency classical and quantum electronics. In a JoFET, the superconducting (zero-resistance) current is controlled by electrostatic gate voltage, which enables the scalability of solid-state quantum computers and the creation of next-generation superconducting integrated circuits (ICs). The development of JoFETs has so far been limited to single devices or a few-transistor circuits due to the lack of reproducible technological platform. Here, we report on technological progress of wafer-scale JoFET fabrication, achieving a 98% yield on a 150 mm wafer platform. Now that the large-scale and reproducible fabrication JoFETs has become feasible, we present behavioral and physics models as the required steps toward the design of novel JoFET-based ICs. Using the presented models, we obtain a good description of experimental data, thus paving the way for the design of next-generation superconducting ICs.

title: Cooperative Control of Photovoltaic and Energy Storage Systems Using BWA-Optimized Input-Output Feedback Linearization
authors: Shenglong Zhou, Zijian Yu, Minfeng Liu, Jianlin Wang
abstract: In photovoltaic and energy storage systems, the nonlinear characteristics of photovoltaic arrays and the dynamic response differences of storage units make it difficult for conventional controllers to achieve both high tracking accuracy and system stability. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a cooperative control strategy based on input-output feedback linearization (IOFL) optimized by the Black-Winged Kite Algorithm (BWA). Specifically, BWA globally optimizes the membership functions and rule weights of a fuzzy MPPT controller, while IOFL decouples the nonlinear interactions between the Boost converter and the bidirectional DC-DC converter. Simulation results show that the proposed BWA-IOFL strategy improves power tracking efficiency by 8.7% under sudden irradiance changes, reduces the power deviation rate to 1.7% under temperature drift conditions, and decreases the SOC fluctuation of the storage unit by more than 15%, verifying its effectiveness, robustness, and application potential for nonlinear PV-storage systems.

title: 37.3 A 2nm All-Digital 14.4Gb/s/pin LPDDR6 PHY with Quarter-Rate Clocking Architecture and Multi-Level FIFO-Based Speculative DFE
authors: Yoonjae Choi, Daero Kim, Myunggon Kim, Sangmin Lee, Hansol Kang, Gwangwon Lee, Yoonhyung Kim, Sungsik Kang, Hyun Cho, Boyoung Kang, Kyoungwon Lee, Jaegeun Song, Jinho Choi, Shinyoung Yi, Billy Koo, Kwanyeob Chae, Hyo-Gyuem Rhew
abstract: This paper presents a 2 nm all-digital LPDDR6 PHY that achieves 14.4 Gb/s/pin, increasing bandwidth while optimizing power. Key innovations include quadrature clocking, with QEC, to reduce jitter sensitivity, and a multi-level FIFO-based speculative DFE to relax timing constraints in standard-cell implementation. An efficiency mode, employing clock gating, dramatically reduces power. A reconfigurable serializer supports both 1:6 and 1:8 DFIs, ensuring high bus efficiency and backward compatibility.

title: Temperature Scalable SPICE Model for Kink Effect in GaN HEMTs for High-Power Applications
authors: Md Hasnain Ansari, Praveen Pal, Avinash Lahgere, Yun-Yueh Hsieh, Min-Li Chou, Yogesh Singh Chauhan
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Emerging Electronics (ICEE)
abstract: The kink effect in GaN (Gallium Nitride) High Electron Mobility Transistors (HEMTs) arises from trap-related device behaviors, leading to a sudden increase in drain current in the output (I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">DS</inf>–V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">DS</inf>) characteristics. Accurate modeling of this effect is essential for reliable circuit design. While previous models based on the Advance SPICE Model (ASM) HEMT framework capture the kink effect, they have limitations in temperature scalability. This work presents an improved ASM-HEMT model with temperature-dependent parameters, enabling accurate prediction of kink onset from −40°C to 150°C. The model, implemented in Verilog-A and validated against both DC and load-pull measurements, demonstrates high accuracy across the wide temperature range, making it suitable for GaN circuit simulation under dynamic thermal conditions.

title: Robust Multimodal Recommendation Based on Heterogeneous Graph Enhancement and Interest Modeling
authors: Lingtong Ma, Ying Ma, Shuxin Ji, Meng You, Yu He, Yafang Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer, Internet of Things and Smart City (CIoTSC)
abstract: To mitigate data sparsity and cold-start challenges in recommendation systems, this work proposes a multimodal heterogeneous graph neural network model that integrates long-term and short-term user interests. First, a multimodal heterogeneous information fusion module is designed to map unstructured visual/textual features to a unified embedding space, suppressing noisy interactions in the heterogeneous network through graph augmentation. Second, a dual-interest learning framework is constructed. A graph convolutional network (GCN) is used to aggregate high-order semantics of the multimodal heterogeneous graph to model long-term interests, while a bidirectional gating unit (Bi-GRU) with temporal attention is employed to capture short-term dynamic preferences. Evaluations on Amazon datasets demonstrate superior performance, with average gains of 15.59% (Recall) and 16.34% (NDCG) over state-of-the-art baselines.

title: Research on the Development of Simulated Traffic Targets based on Radar Reflectivity Testing and Camera Feature Capture
authors: Ailing Deng, Xin Bi, Kongjian Qin, Haitao Zhou, Dewei Shen
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer, Internet of Things and Smart City (CIoTSC)
abstract: This study, in response to the requirements of autonomous driving safety testing,independently developed a low-cost, lightweight, cloud-controllable simulated traffic participant system (including dummies and dummies), focusing on addressing the high cost and insufficient localization of existing 4A dummies and dummies. The system features a carbon fiber chassis and EPE cushioned body design, enabling single-person transportation, crash-resistant and quick-change functions, and supports fully unmanned test scenarios through a 5G cloud control architecture. To verify the system performance, this paper conducted two key tests: radar cross-sectional area (RCS) and camera recall rate: based on 4D millimeter-wave radar, compared with the 4A dummy vehicle according to ISO 19206-2/1-2018 standard, the results showed that the RCS values of Tongji self-developed target objects at multiple angles met the national -standard, and the performance at some angles was better than that of the 4A product; The YOLOv8 algorithm was used to evaluate the detection results. The recall rate of the self-developed dummy reached 98.2% (higher than 92.0% of the 4A dummy), and the recall rate of the self-developed dummy was 70.7% (close to 78.3% of the 4A dummy), verifying that the system could meet the requirements of high-fidelity simulation. This study provides an economical and efficient localized solution for autonomous driving safety testing, breaks foreign technological monopolies, and promotes the standardization of intelligent transportation systems.

title: Modeling of Low-Frequency Noise Related to Conduction Mechanisms in Amorphous Oxide Semiconductor Thin-Film Transistors
authors: Minxi Cai, Wei Zhong, Jianhua Cai, Piaorong Xu
abstract: We develop a low-frequency noise (LFN) model for amorphous oxide semiconductor thin-film transistors (AOS TFTs) that accounts for the band-gap density of states (DOSs) of AOSs. The model uses a charge-based approach for calculating the normalized drain current power spectral density (PSD). By comparing the model with experimental results, we show that it is applicable to various AOS TFTs. The dominant noise source is carrier number fluctuation with correlated mobility for both AOS band-gap states (at low gate voltages) and oxide traps (at high gate voltages). AOS TFTs with high-k gate dielectrics may also require including additional mobility fluctuation (Hooge noise) due to the higher noise level in the subthreshold regime. The channel charge-based DC and noise models are strongly correlated in AOS TFTs, including for band-gap states in AOS.

title: Research on Variable Frequency Impedance Model of Electromechanical Parallel System and Application
authors: Lu Liu, Guanglin Sha, Yao Zhang, Yunzhao Wu, Jingwei Zhao, Shu Liu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Systems, Smart Grid, and Artificial Intelligence (PSGAI)
abstract: For a multi-inverters parallel-connected based system, if the power loop is controlled by a droop regulator, the low frequency domain specification can be analyzed by power loop when omitting the voltage loop and current loop. Its high frequency domain specification can be gotten with a impedance model at a steady state operation point, and above all things the system frequency variation created by droop control does not exist in the model and some low frequency domain information will be lost. In this paper, for a diesel synchronous generator formed microgrid that features frequency droop characteristic, a variable frequency impedance model (VFIM) is proposed. The impedance model is established according to the synchronous generator traditional control scheme and its full order state-space model. Combined with the VFIM of the current controlled inverter, the system frequency variable is global and exert impact on all the parallel connected inverters. The frequency domain features and then the mechanism of electromechanical parallel system damp weakening is analyzed based on this proposed model, especially the influence of external frequency disturbance on the impedance of the inverter. The proposed impedance model were validated through MATLAB/Simulink simulations. It is indicated that increasing the number of inverters reduces system damping and may lead to oscillations. The model is useful for analyzing stability of islanded microgrids with frequency fluctuations and supporting the optimal operation of electromechanical parallel systems.

title: Optimized Scheduling Method Incorporating Aggregated HVAC Frequency Response
authors: Wanyi Zhang, Xiaohui Zhao
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Systems, Smart Grid, and Artificial Intelligence (PSGAI)
abstract: Heating, Ventilation and Air Conditioning (HVAC) loads are large-scale and fast-responding, making them suitable load-side resources for primary frequency regulation. This paper integrates aggregated HVAC loads into a Unit Commitment (UC) scheduling framework with frequency stability constraints. The proposed model explicitly captures key chiller operating characteristics through performance correction factors related to cooling water temperature, chilled water supply temperature, and part-load ratio, whose values are derived from standard chiller performance curves in EnergyPlus. The total building cooling demand is treated as a system-level optimization variable within physical operating limits, enabling coordinated scheduling of generation and load-side reserves. Simulation results show that introducing aggregated HVAC reduces the frequency regulation burden on synchronous generators by approximately 34.80 %, while lowering total system operating cost by 14.38 %. Furthermore, optimizing cooling demand within chiller constraints increases the available frequency regulation reserve from HVAC by 29 %, improving reserve utilization and enhancing the economic efficiency of frequency regulation under high renewable penetration.

title:
A Fractional-N Digital PLL with a Supply-Insensitive DTC Achieving −62dBc Spur and 69fs Jitter Under 10mVpp Sinusoidal DTC Supply Ripple and 6.2mV
                    <sub>rms</sub>
                    DTC Supply Noise
authors: Damiano Fagotti, Riccardo Moleri, Michele Rossoni, Daniele Lodi Rizzini, Pietro Salvi, Stefano Gallucci, Giovanni Rocco Trotta, Andrea Leonardo Lacaita, Simone Mattia Dartizio, Salvatore Levantino
abstract: A 28 nm fractional-N digital PLL with a supply-insensitive variable-slope digital-to-time converter (DTC) is presented. Supply insensitivity is achieved by a background calibration loop, without affecting DTC linearity or noise. Measured results demonstrate 65.6fs jitter upon 6.2 mVrms supply noise and -62dBc spurs under 10 mV pp supply-ripple over 10k-to-50 MHz disturbance frequency range. The PLL covers 8.75-to-10.25GHz tuning range, while achieving a -251 dB jitter-power figure of merit at 22.5 mW power.

title: IWAIN 2025 - Author Index
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 2nd International Workshop on Artificial Intelligence for Neuroscience (IWAIN)

title: Author Index
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Network Systems (CINS)

title: Vision sync: Enhancing Accessibility with a Multi-Function Assistive Device for the Visually Impaired
authors: S. Nithya Roopa
publish: 2025 First International Conference of Advances in Engineering and Computing Technologies for Sustainable Development (AECTSD)
abstract: Navigating the physical world can be a formidable challenge for the blind and elderly population. They face mobility constraints, a heightened risk of accidents, increased isolation, and a loss of independence. To address these challenges, we propose a novel solution that aims to empower the visually impaired through advanced technology. The proposed work endeavors to provide individuals with visual impairments with a device that enhances their ability to navigate, detect obstacles, identify people, and comprehend their surroundings. By alerting users through haptic feedback or audio cues about obstacles in their path, our device promotes safer mobility, fosters greater independence, and mitigates the risk of accidents. Beyond these practical benefits, our solution enriches users' spatial awareness and instill confidence in their ability to move confidently in their environment.

title: NLP-Powered Test Case Generation from Function Description – A Cross-Language Approach
authors: Jonah Varghese Kachirackal, Vijayakumar Balakrishnan
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Network Systems (CINS)
abstract: This paper presents a practical framework for building an AI-Based Automated Test Case Generator capable of handling multiple programming languages including Python, JavaScript and Rust. Traditional test case generation methods are limited by manual effort, language constraints, and poor adaptability to real-world codebases. To address this, our system leverages a fine-tuned transformer model (StarCoderBase-1B) using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), guided by structured inputs.We incorporate both Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs) and Control Flow Graphs (CFGs) to enhance the model’s syntactic and semantic reasoning capabilities. ASTs represent code structure, while CFGs allow understanding of control logic, loops, and branching. Test quality is further optimized through reinforcement learning and mutation testing techniques, ensuring fault detection and coverage improvements.Training is performed using datasets such as The Stack, HumanEval, and CodeNet, reformatted into structured JSONL entries with aligned code, control graphs, and test cases. We conclude by highlighting current limitations, including zero-shot generalization and dynamic input handling, and suggest directions for future work in AI-powered quality assurance.

title: 16.9 A 96.6% Single-Mode Hybrid Dual-Path Buck-Boost Converter with Conduction Loss Reduction through Conversion-Ratio-Based Adaptive 3-Phase Control
authors: Minsu Kim, Yunho Lee, Hyunjun Park, Hongseok Kim, Woojoong Jung, Minwoo Kim, Junhyun Bae, Hyoung-Seok Oh, Hwayeal Yu, Hyung-Min Lee
abstract: This paper presents a 3-phase dual-path buck-boost converter that effectively reduces conduction loss by combining a dual-path topology with adaptive 3-phase control. The proposed control technique minimizes inductor current ripple, which allows the use of a low-inductance inductor with low DCR, while reducing the inductor current. Fabricated in a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.13 \mu \mathrm{m}$</tex> CMOS process, the converter achieves a high peak-efficiency of 96.6% with a small <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.92 \text{mm}^{3}$</tex> inductor and 0402-size flying capacitors.

title: Session 10 Overview: Digital Processing and Circuit Techniques
