abstract: While electroencephalography (EEG)-based user authentication has demonstrated strong potential in controlled laboratory conditions, its reliability in more realistic settings remains underexplored. In this work, we investigate EEG-based user authentication in realistic interactive scenarios involving cognitively and behaviorally rich tasks. We collected EEG data from subjects under two conditions: solo reading aloud and two-person dialog-based games. These scenarios represent practical, everyday activities where users produce speech and engage in turn-taking interactions, introducing non-stationarity, muscle artifacts, and attention shifts that challenge conventional EEG-based models. To evaluate the user authentication performance under these challenges, we systematically apply various deep learning backbone models and loss functions under a fair and consistent experimental protocol. Specifically, we first divide the whole EEG signal into short windows with pre-defined length and stride. For each window, a time-frequency representation is computed using a continuous wavelet transform, resulting in a sequence of 2D time-frequency maps that capture the non-stationary characteristics of EEG signals. These maps are then fed into various backbone models, such as long short term memory (LSTM) and convolutional neural network (CNN), which are trained to extract robust identity-discriminative features. To further enhance inter-subject separability, we employ the Softmax loss or ArcFace loss during training. Experimental results demonstrate that even under dynamic and less controlled conditions, EEG signals retain individual-specific patterns, yielding high authentication accuracy. These findings highlight the feasibility of extending EEG-based biometrics to more natural environments.

title: 28.1 Importance of GaN for 5G and Solid-State mm-Wave Circuits of the Future
authors: Umesh K. Mishra, Christopher J. Clymore, Emre Akso, Matthew Guidry
abstract: GaN has played an important role in both RF and power devices. GaN made the rapid development and deployment of 5G base stations possible with use of its Ga-polar face. Meanwhile, the N-polar face of GaN has shown great promise through current research, with record power and efficiency at W-band. The benefits of N-polar GaN for systems include a smaller size with fewer power amplifier chips combined due to its higher power density, and lower cost of operation with its improved efficiency.

title: A 5.7mW@0.55V-to-50mW@0.9V Deeply Power-Scalable Reconfigurable Series-Resonance/Class-F VCO with Mutual-Inductance Self-Cancellation and Hybrid 8-Shaped Coupling Techniques
authors: Juntao Lan, Wei Deng, Haikun Jia, Shiwei Zhang, Zhihua Wang, Baoyong Chi
abstract: This work presents a 28nm deeply power-scalable reconfigurable series-resonance/ClassF VCO with mutual-inductance self-cancellation and hybrid 8-shaped coupling techniques, achieving power scalability from 5.7 to 93.1 mW. By switching between parallel-resonance (Class-F) and series-resonance modes, the VCO achieves −149 dBc/Hz PN and 192.8 dBc/Hz FoM at a 10 MHz offset. This design enables flexible PN-power scalability for Wi-Fi 7, IoT, and wearable devices.

title: A 1.6-to-3.8GHz Reconfigurable FMCW Radar SoC with 81.5% Relative-Bandwidth PLL for Real-Time Life Detection in Disaster Response
authors: Renjie Fu, Linxu Shi, Fanxun Cai, Yunqi Yang, Hao Lei, Zhigang Fu, Yilin Xu, Pengcheng Wang, Ziyang Li, Shixuan Wang, Junkai Jiao, Dingqi Liang, Lianbo Wu, Guangzhong Zhang, Naike Du, Zijie Wang, Yucong Gu, Xiuzhu Ye, Xiao Fang, Weisheng Zhao, Hui Zhang
abstract: A 1.6-to-3.8GHz FMCW radar SoC for real-time non-line-of-sight life detection is implemented in 40nm CMOS. An 81.5% BW ring-VCO PLL with DTC achieves 29.6kHz frequency error and 0.78-to-400MHz/µs chirp slopes. The TX integrates a 13.43dBm PA, while the RXRF features 8.1dB NF, baseband chain with 100dB-rejection SC-HPF at 1kHz and CT Δ-∑ ADC. A RISC-V core with FPU/VPU enables DSP, detecting people behind 6 walls and vital signs behind 2, occupying 7.65mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> and consuming 0.395W.

title: Research on AI-Driven Autonomous Optimization for Database Appliances
authors: Huina Guo, Junjie Li, Zhao Wang, Bing Li, Yida Shen
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer, Internet of Things and Smart City (CIoTSC)
abstract: With the explosive growth of data scale, traditional database management systems face severe challenges. Database appliances, while integrating hardware and software resources, still suffer from strong dependence on manual optimization and low resource utilization efficiency. This paper presents an autonomous optimization framework built on machine learning and deep reinforcement learning algorithms, achieving adaptive adjustment of resource allocation and query optimization. Experimental data shows that this framework improves performance by 42.7% in TPC-H benchmark tests, outperforms mainstream autonomous databases by 28.0%, reduces manual intervention time by 93.2%, and shortens system recovery time to 98 seconds. The framework demonstrates optimal performance in medium-scale deployments while facing scalability challenges in large distributed systems. This research pioneers a new path for the complete autonomous evolution of database appliances and has broad application prospects.

title: 14.1 THz-TSI: A 0.33pJ/b 264Gb/s Through-Silicon Interconnect Module for 3D Integration Utilizing Terahertz Coupling
authors: Chen Jiang, Xiaodi Feng, Xiaohan Shen, Chixiao Chen, Qi Liu, Ming Liu, Ningsheng Xu
abstract: A through-silicon interconnect module for 3D integration utilizing THz coupling is presented, which achieves a record high data rate of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$264 ~\text{Gb} / \mathrm{s}$</tex> and efficiency of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.33 \text{pJ} / \mathrm{b}$</tex>. Both bidirectional point-to-point link and broadcast mode are successfully demonstrated. This scheme offers highly competitive bandwidth and density with significantly simpler fabrication and lower cost. Furthermore, its high flexibility enables real-time adjustable interconnect topology to effectively reduce latency.

title: Lightweight Blockchain-Integrated Medical Image Encryption Scheme with Modified Chaotic Tent Mapping in the Edge-Cloud Environments
authors: Gnanajeyaraman Rajaram, U Arul, M Ramamoorthy, A Manimaran, Carmel Mary Belinda M J
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Network Systems (CINS)
abstract: With telemedicine and cloud-based diagnosing rising rapidly, the need to maintain medical imaging data as secure and confidential is increasing in importance. Due to excessive redundancy and pixel correlation in biomedical images, they are not always effectively preserved with the help of the traditional cryptographic techniques. The present work offers a secure method to encrypt medical photos based on the chaotic systems and blockchain applications that develop an effective and secure way of protecting medical images stored in clouds. There exist the Modified Tent Map and the Logistic Map, which are two chaotic models used to work and to be tested to get the estimation of their efficiency in terms of encrypting the grey level MRI brain slices. To analyze the two models, we apply standard statistical parameters to include NPCR (Number of Pixels Change Rate), UACI (Unified Average Changing Intensity), and PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio). To check the utility in real life in terms of such aspects as histogram analysis and such performance measures as execution time and memory consumption. The tests prove that both systems are well encrypted. Entropy is performing near perfection (8.0), and the NPCR is greater than 99 per cent, which implies that differential attacks are highly difficult to break. The Tent Map has the better UACI (33.6% vs. 31.2%) and PSNR (48.1 dB vs 46.9 dB) when compared to the Logistic Map. It is also quicker at encrypting information (0.135 s versus 0.158 s). The logistic map, however, uses less memory, which is a beneficial option when it comes to lightweight programs. The suggested dual-chaotic encryption design provides a non-hackable, highly fast and reversible fix to encrypt medical images on the cloud and at distributed healthcare where a massive speed of encryption is required.

title: AI-Augmented Virtual Screening for COVID-19 Antivirals
authors: Priyanshi V. Mulwani, Shripad Joshi, C Ambhika, Surendarkumar S, Neeraj Varshney, Karthikayen A
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Network Systems (CINS)
abstract: The ongoing global threat of COVID-19 continues to require speedy and dependable antiviral drug discovery techniques. Traditional drug-screening protocols are time-consuming and very much limited in predictive shortcomings, while AI-driven methods, although computationally intensive, can be lacking in molecular binding's structural elements. This research introduces an AI-facilitated virtual screening strategy marrying machine learning–based affinity prediction with structure-based molecular docking for identifying potent inhibitors of the SARS-CoV-2 main protease (Mpro). A curated dataset of 4,200 drug-like compounds, sourced from ChEMBL and PubChem and filtered using Lipinski’s Rule of Five, was utilized for model training and validation. A Random Forest ensemble that was trained using molecular descriptors had a prediction accuracy of 91.3%, outperforming both traditional docking (68.4%) and AI-exclusive models (82.7%) in prediction precision, valid binding hits, and screening efficiency. The double-score integration and double-validation strategy successfully minimized false positives by synergizing statistical learning and structural validation. The hybrid approach improves computational efficiency and interpretability, with a reproducible and extensible methodology towards antiviral discovery. Future developments involve integrating dynamic protein modeling, ADMET profiling, and improved compound diversity to further enhance biological relevance and predictive robustness.

title: DeepShieldFed: Securing Face Templates via Deep Cancelable Transforms and Federated Learning
authors: Amber Hayat, Md Atiqur Rahman Ahad, Vireshwar Kumar, Ashok Kumar Bhateja
publish: 2025 IEEE International Joint Conference on Biometrics (IJCB)
abstract: The adoption of facial recognition technology for identity verification has seen significant growth in recent years. While state-of-the-art (SOTA) facial recognition systems demonstrate high accuracy, the biometric features extracted and stored in system databases are inherently privacy-sensitive. If compromised, this data poses a serious threat to user privacy. To address this challenge, we propose a novel federated learning-based cancelable template protection framework that combines secure, user-specific transformations with collaborative training. In our approach, each user’s face features are first converted into a cancelable template, revocable representation, and then used to train a shared model through federated learning, which enables multiple clients to jointly update a global model without sharing their raw or transformed biometric data. We evaluate our method across key security properties, including unlinkability, revocability, and resilience to inversion attacks, in compliance with the ISO/IEC 30136 standard for biometric performance and protection. Experiments conducted on the large-scale CelebA and LFW dataset using modern facial recognition architectures demonstrate that our approach achieves competitive recognition performance while significantly enhancing security, while preserving recognition performance.

title: Long–Short Term Spatial–Temporal Masked Transformer for Traffic Flow Prediction
authors: Ping Zhang, Zongwen Liu, Wei Liang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer, Internet of Things and Smart City (CIoTSC)
abstract: Accurate traffic flow forecasting has emerged as a vital function within intelligent transportation systems, offering considerable potential for real-world deployment.Recently, graph transformer-based models have gained widespread attention for their ability to effectively extract features from both sequential and graph-structured data. Existing methods typically apply transformers separately to capture dependencies in traffic flow data. However, two major limitations remain. In temporal modeling, these methods overlook the distinction between long-term trends and short-term temporal dependencies. Additionally, realworld traffic networks exhibit sparsity and locality, meaning that not all nodes have direct or significant influence relationships. Yet, most spatial models assume potential dependencies between all node pairs, which may introduce spurious associations and ultimately reduce prediction accuracy.To overcome these challenges, we introduce LSMFormer, a long-short term temporal gating mechanism prior to the temporal transformer to capture both persistent temporal trends and transient variations in traffic flow patterns. Additionally, we design a strong-correlation mask matrix and a self-learned mask matrix within the spatial transformer to filter out weakly correlated node pairs. We conduct experiments on public datasets, and the results showed that our model had better accuracy than other baselines.

title: Knowledge Assistant for Joint Utility: A Multi-Agent LLM-Driven Conversational System for Automated Task Execution
authors: Krish Gopani, Shruti Shah, Abhay Mathur, Naman Lakhani, Nilesh Rathod, Aruna Gawade
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Computational Intelligence and Network Systems (CINS)
abstract: The integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) into developer workflows has redefined automation, yet most systems remain limited to executing direct terminal commands. A critical limitation in existing tools is the lack of adaptive AI systems that cater to both technical and non-technical users. Knowledge Assistant for Joint Utility advances this capability by enabling not only command execution but also intelligent explanation, code comprehension, and handling of complex, context-dependent tasks through file analysis. The system functions as a fully embedded LLM within the terminal environment. This assistant introduces a modular, agent-based architecture that supports debugging, screen processing, code editing, web-assisted installations, and dynamic project integration through a FastAPI backend and Java-based graphical interface. The system’s core innovation is its autonomous self-customization engine, where the Builder Agent interprets natural language requests to generate, implement, and integrate new functionalities into the assistant’s own codebase, allowing the system to dynamically evolve. Specialized agents—including Scraper, LLM, Builder, and Assistant—coordinate to deliver iterative, context-aware development and intelligent task handling. Evaluation across 124 installations yielded a Task Success Rate of 88.75% using GPT-4o and a Completion Rate of 93.45%, outperforming other LLMs such as Gemini and DeepSeek. These results highlight the system’s potential as a multi-agent computational framework that integrates conversational interaction with autonomous software engineering.

title: Sustainable Smart Manufacturing: Leveraging AI and Blockchain for Resource-Efficient Metal Recovery and Supply Chain Integrity
authors: Manoj Srivastava, Nithin Subba Rao
publish: 2025 First International Conference of Advances in Engineering and Computing Technologies for Sustainable Development (AECTSD)
abstract: The concept of sustainable smart manufacturing has become a major cause of concern as industries seek to lessen the effects on the environment and still ensure the efficiency of operations. This paper conducts empirical research on the potential to use Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Blockchain technologies in collaboration to facilitate the metal recovery process in a resource-efficient manner and guarantee the integrity of the supply chain that cannot be modified. The study tests the effectiveness of AI-based predictive recovery model and blockchain-supported provenance tracking using a hybrid dataset gathered in industrial metal recovery units, recycling facilities, and supply chains logs. RF, XGBoost, and LSTM models of machine learning were trained to maximize metal recovery yield, energy usage and process efficiency. An end-to-end supply chain validation was done by building a private blockchain architecture. Findings indicate that AI models have been able to enhance recovery efficiency to a maximum of 18 percent whereas, blockchain minimized discrepancies associated with traceability by 92 percent. The integrated model is much more effective than conventional practices and offers a roadmap of scalable metal supply chains that would be sustainable and transparent. The next uses can be the cross-border tracking of materials and the optimization of a supply chain in terms of carbon.

title: 17.3 ARIES and REGULUS: A Unified and Scalable hardware–software co-designed NPU SoC Family for On-Device and On-Premises Multimodal Inference
authors: D. Shin, H. Yang, S. Jeon, J. Park, S. Han, J. Park, J. Lee, J. Kim, H. Kim, Y. Oh, M. Kim, C. Jung, W. Kim, S. Kim, H. Jeong, G. Kim, K. Lee, G. Song, Y. Min, C. Song, A. Kanybek, Y. Jung, J. Song, S. Cho, H. Na, J. Park, D. Si, B. Lee, B. Park, H. Jeon
abstract: We present a scalable NPU architecture, proven in two SoCs (ARIES and REGULUS), designed to efficiently execute diverse AI workloads from vision to large language models. The architecture scales seamlessly across cores, clusters, and chips via a unified programming model. Our holistic hardware-software co-design preserves model accuracy through a bound- and distribution-aware mixed-precision quantization scheme, while efficiently handling complex activation functions via a non-uniform piecewise-linear method. Silicon measurements demonstrate near-linear performance scaling and high power and bandwidth efficiency on both vision and language workloads.

title: Parameter Configuration for Advanced Adiabatic Compressed Air Energy Storage Systems Based on the Adaptive Step-Size ADMM Algorithm
authors: Kai Wang, Tianyu Li, Xue Chen, Sen Cui, Tingxiang Liu, Laijun Chen
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Systems, Smart Grid, and Artificial Intelligence (PSGAI)
abstract: The uncertainty of renewable energy output and the nonlinear characteristics of the Advanced Adiabatic Compressed Air Energy Storage (AA-CAES) system pose challenges to the design of the system. This paper proposes a parameter configuration method for AA-CAES based on the Adaptive Step-size Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (AS-ADMM) algorithm. Firstly, a thermodynamic model of the AA-CAES system suitable for optimization problems is established. Subsequently, an AA-CAES parameter configuration method based on the adaptive step-size Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers algorithm is proposed. In the optimization problem, the uncertainty of renewable energy output is represented by a random probability set. The optimization problem is decoupled into two sub-problems respectively related to the electrical and thermal properties of AA-CAES through the equation relationship between the system's electrical power and thermal power. The AS-ADMM method is employed to solve the thermal and electrical balance problems during the AA-CAES configuration process to find the optimal solution. Finally, the feasibility of the model and method was verified through case analysis, proving that the wind abandonment rate of the system is 5.56% compared to approximately 22% without energy storage.

title: AI and ML-Driven Automated Cluster and Angular-Based Energy Proficient Trusted Routing Protocol for IoT-Enabled Mobile Ad-Hoc Network
authors: Prabakaran S, Senthil Kumar A
publish: 2025 First International Conference of Advances in Engineering and Computing Technologies for Sustainable Development (AECTSD)
abstract: The dynamic and decentralized nature of Mobile Ad-Hoc Networks (MANETs), especially when combined with Internet of Things (IoT) devices, imposes severe constraints on energy efficiency, trust management, and routing stability. The routing protocols in place are not usually designed to sustain optimal performance in high mobility and malicious activity. To handle these issues, in this paper, an AI and ML-led Automated Cluster and Angular-Based Energy Proficient Trusted Routing Protocol (TEPR-ML) specific to IoT-enabled MANET setting is presented. The protocol proposed incorporates a fuzzy-based improved clustering mechanism with hybrid K-Means DBSCAN adaptive cluster formation with cluster head (CH) election based on energy, trust, mobility, and angular proximity. A LSTM-based angular topology predictor is used in order to predict link stability and aid in proactive path choice. A composite scoring policy (i.e., comprising of residual energy, trust metrics, and angular stability) is used to optimize routing decisions, which are further refined by reinforcement learning agents. The protocol is also designed to include a Bayesian trust model with SHAP-based interpretability in order to identify and isolate malicious nodes. Large-scale experiments with NS-3 prove that TEPRML is superior in various performance measures compared to traditional protocols, including AODV, DSR, and T-AOMDV. It attains a ratio of packets delivered up to 98.3, saves energy at rate of 22 %and also detects the trust with accuracy more than 94 predicaments in adversarial conditions. The findings validate the resiliency, flexibility, and safety of the suggested structure when operating in high-mobility, resource-restricted IoT MANET.

title: An Improved YOLO11-Based Power Transmission Line Detection Method
authors: Zonghui Pan, Wenbiao Cai, Nan Li, Bo Zhang, Yuwei Liang, Honghong Sun, Taowei Wang, Tianhao Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Systems, Smart Grid, and Artificial Intelligence (PSGAI)
abstract: Given the frequent occurrence of external damage incidents on transmission lines, and the challenges in detecting such hazards—where targets resemble surrounding background features, exhibit significant scale variations, or are partially obscured—leading to false positives and false negatives, this paper proposes an enhanced YOLO11 algorithm for detecting external damage hazards in power grids. This algorithm employs deep separable convolutions to enhance the model's multi-scale feature extraction capabilities for detecting potential external damage hazards. The introduction of the C3k2 module improves computational efficiency, enabling more effective feature propagation and reducing the impact of background factors and occlusions on detection performance. Concurrently, the adoption of SPPF and C2PSA modules increases the model's focus on critical image regions, thereby improving the accuracy of bounding box localisation when objects are partially obscured. Experimental results demonstrate that this approach outperforms other mainstream algorithms, achieving a mean average precision (mAP@0.5) of 94.8% without substantially increasing parameter complexity. This validates the method's effectiveness, providing algorithmic support for edge detection of mechanical external breach hazard targets in complex scenarios.

title: Improved Optimisation in Image Recognition System for Crime Detection using the amalgamation of Faster R-CNN and Inception-v3 algorithms
authors: Diana Grace Thomas, Anithaashri T.P
publish: 2025 First International Conference of Advances in Engineering and Computing Technologies for Sustainable Development (AECTSD)
abstract: In recent days, the image recognition system for crime detection is poor in extracting features from the blurred images. The number of crime occurrences are not identified with exact victims due to the lack of proofs. The images captured through CCTV and other variety of cameras are not giving the high accuracy in extracting features, thus provides the latency in identifying the crime. The proposed system designed to identify the crime in a stipulated time and also helps in extracting features with more accuracy. By using Faster Region based Convolutional Neural Network (R-CNN) algorithm and Inception-v3 algorithm, the image recognition has been improvised with high accuracy compared with traditional methods. The algorithms tested over 20 iterations with variety of images and inferred with high accuracy of 86% in identifying the crime with extracted features.

title: Optimal Configuration of Hydrogen Production and Storage Systems in Renewable Energy Clusters Considering Ammonia Plant Demand Profiles
authors: Xue Chen, Hanchen Liu, Kai Wang, Yingcong Sun, Zhimei Cao, Laijun Chen
publish: 2025 International Conference on Power Systems, Smart Grid, and Artificial Intelligence (PSGAI)
abstract: Driven by carbon neutrality goals, hydrogen has emerged as a critical carrier for long-duration energy storage, yet high capital costs remain a barrier for individual renewable energy stations. To address this, this paper proposes an optimal configuration framework for a shared hydrogen production and storage system (HPSS) where a cluster of renewable energy stations jointly invests in facilities to supply downstream ammonia plants. A two-stage stochastic programming model is established to determine the optimal capacity configuration and operation strategy while handling renewable output and price uncertainties. The model can be solved via Bender's dual decomposition methods. Distinctively, the study introduces a flexible hydrogen demand model based on the Haber-Bosch process, which permits a specific range of supply fluctuations rather than requiring a rigid constant supply. Case study results demonstrate that considering the flexible demand characteristics of ammonia plants reduces the required hydrogen storage capacity by approximately 10%. Furthermore, the cooperative mode increases the overall net revenue by approximately 15% compared to independent operations by leveraging the spatiotemporal complementarity of wind and solar output. This work provides a viable pathway for enhancing renewable energy accommodation and improving system economics, offering valuable insights for the evolution of future power systems.

title: A Binary-Weighted Switched-Capacitor Gate Driver IC for Overcoming Trade-Offs Between Driving Loss and Delay Time with Gate-Current Feedback Achieving 85% Driving Loss Reduction
authors: Kohei Horii, Koutaro Miyazaki, Shusuke Kawai, Hiroaki Ishihara
abstract: A binary-weighted switched-capacitor gate driver IC achieving up to 85% reduction in driving loss (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{E}_{\text{drv }}$</tex>) is presented. Four binary-weighted flying capacitors enable nine-level gate-voltage segmentation, enhancing charge supply capability. Adaptive state interval time control based on gate-current feedback mitigates the trade-off between the switching time of the gate-voltage (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$t_{\text{sw}}$</tex>) and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{E}_{\text{drv}}$</tex>, reducing tsw by 19% at equal <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{E}_{\text{drv}}$</tex>, and reducing <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{E}_{\text{drv}}$</tex> by 9% at equal <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{t}_{\text{sw}}$</tex>.

title: Octree-Based Hierarchical Genetic Algorithm for Efficient 3D Coverage Path Planning
authors: Keyu Chen, Jun Han
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer, Internet of Things and Smart City (CIoTSC)
abstract: Automated drone inspection is increasingly critical for operating and maintaining large-scale infrastructure, creating a pressing need for intelligent three-dimensional path planning around complex structures such as transmission towers. Traditional two-dimensional planning methods often fall short in ensuring full coverage and flight safety in three-dimensional environments. This paper proposes an integrated approach combining octree-based multi-resolution modeling with an improved genetic algorithm for hierarchical 3D path optimization. Environmental-aware viewpoint sampling is first conducted, followed by octree-based hierarchical spatial segmentation. A global inspection sequence is planned, followed by the calculation of spatial connection points between regions. A genetic algorithm is then employed to optimize the local paths connecting these points, ultimately forming a smooth and executable global trajectory. Results demonstrate that the method computes paths efficiently (typically under 10 seconds) and achieves near-complete coverage with sufficient viewpoints, outperforming existing approaches. Although the resulting path is approximately twice as long as those generated by methods based on the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) planner, it remains operationally acceptable while significantly enhancing safety and flyability. The proposed solution offers high versatility and practicality for autonomous drone inspections, promising to improve automation and reduce labor costs and risks.

title: Applications and Challenges of Multi-Core Scheduling in Intelligent Automotive Systems
authors: Yaxin Wei, Nandong Li, Yisheng An, Shumei Liu, Yonghui Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Parallel and Distributed Systems
abstract: Recent advancements in computing and autonomous driving technologies have led to the integration of new functionalities into intelligent automotive, such as environmental perception, path planning, assisted driving, and entertainment services. This integration requires the rapid processing of critical tasks, including collision detection, emergency braking, lane keeping, and Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication, exceeding past functional demands. Consequently, high-performance multi-core processors have emerged as the preferred hardware solution due to their superior processing speeds, energy efficiency, and parallel task execution capabilities. This paper explores various applications of multi core processors in intelligent automotive systems, systematically reviewing recent advancements in multi-core scheduling methods. It categorizes and analyzes approaches to task loading, task migration, efficiency improvement, safety assurance, communication, and resource contention in both general and automotive contexts. To objectively assess these methods, the paper establishes reference standards for evaluating scheduling methods and system architectures and provides analytical methods aligned with these standards. Finally, the paper discusses future challenges, considers trends in intelligent automotive systems and multi-core processors, and offers recommendations for future research in this field.

title: AVO-QP: Task-Adaptive Real-Time Obstacle Avoidance for Redundant Manipulators on Edge Platforms
authors: Fang Peng, Yifei Li, Honghui Zhang, Feilong Wang, Wen Qi, Hang Su, Samer Alfayad
abstract: We address real-time obstacle avoidance for redundant manipulators where tracking and safety constraints can conflict and render quadratic programs (QPs) infeasible. We propose AVO-QP, a sensor-guided velocity-level planner that fuses RGB-D depth with learned detection to maintain situational awareness, and executes the full perception–planning–control loop on edge platforms without centralized servers. A task-adaptive avoidance vector <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$U_{\rm{avo}}$</tex-math></inline-formula> modifies the translational tracking constraint when obstacles enter a safety margin, reducing tracking–avoidance conflicts while preserving QP feasibility. A dual-objective cost balances joint-velocity magnitude and inter-step variation, and a projected primal–dual RNN solves the QP at control rate to produce smooth, feasible joint commands. Experiments on a 7-DOF Franka Panda (simulation) and a 6-DOF UR5 (hardware) with static and dynamic obstacles show an overall success rate of 84.09%, average planning times of 19.0–20.3 ms on edge devices, and terminal position errors down to 0.0001 m in static scenes while respecting distance constraints. These results suggest that AVO-QP enables responsive manipulation in cluttered workcells under tight latency budgets.

title: Control-Oriented Modeling of Comparator Overdrive Delay for Stabilizing High-Frequency Current-Mode DC-DC Converters
authors: Guanyu Qian, Al-Thaddeus Avestruz, Xiaofan Cui
abstract: The comparator is one of the most intensively used yet often inadequately modeled components in power electronics. This paper presents a control-oriented behavioral model of the comparator overdrive delay (COD) and leverages it to address the stability issues of high-frequency current-mode DC-DC converters caused by current-sensing interference. This model links the comparator's intrinsic dynamics to its nonlinear filtering behavior, matching closely the experimental delay-overdrive characteristics while maintaining analytical simplicity and design intuitions. Building on this model, a COD conditioning approach is proposed, which exploits the intrinsic comparator dynamics to reshape sensor interference and thereby stabilize current-mode converters. An analytical stability condition is then derived through a Lur'e system representation. This theory is validated on a 2.3 MHz buck converter prototype, where commercial off-the-shelf comparators are leveraged to eliminate the instability, manifested as inductor current subharmonic oscillations. The results confirm the practicality of the COD conditioning method, highlighting its advantages over conventional linear filtering techniques, and reveal a new design insight: a properly engineered comparator delay can enhance the resilience of current-mode power converters without sacrificing transient speed.

title: Traffic Characterization of Event-Triggered Multiagent Systems Under FDI Attacks
authors: Tao Chen, Wentuo Fang, Wenfeng Hu, Gui Gui, Chunhua Yang
abstract: In this article, we investigate the triggering behaviors of periodic event-triggered multiagent systems (MASs) under multiplicative false data injection (FDI) attacks. An abstraction-based traffic model is established to characterize all possible triggering behaviors under arbitrary initial states, including the minimum interevent time (MIET) and the transition relations among IETs. We further answer the following two questions: 1) how FDI attacks affect the MIET and 2) how to select the sampling period for the anomalous MIET detection. As a potential application scenario, a behavior-based anomaly detection algorithm is developed based on the proposed traffic model to identify anomalous triggering behaviors caused by attacks. Simulations demonstrate the effectiveness and practical application of the proposed results.

title: Adversarial SQLi Detection Using Character-Level CNN and Reinforcement Learning
authors: Fucai Yu, Xusheng Li, Yang Wu, Ziqiang Chang, Gaolei Fei, Xuemeng Zhai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Adversarial SQL injection (SQLi) refers to the process in which attackers dynamically modify their attack strategies based on feedback from the target Web Application Firewall (WAF) in an attempt to bypass it. The emergence of automated adversarial SQLi tools in recent years, such as AdvSQLi, RAT, and GPTFuzzer, demonstrates that adversarial SQLi has become a critical method for vulnerability detection and SQLi execution. These tools' intelligent nature poses a significant threat to existing SQLi defense mechanisms. Payloads subjected to adversarial mutation can effectively deceive machine learning-based WAFs, while rule-based WAFs are more susceptible to having vulnerabilities discovered. To address this issue, we propose a hybrid model based on enhanced Character-Level CNN (CLCNN) and Reinforcement Learning (RL), which detects malicious patterns through multi-scale feature extraction by CLCNN from a pattern-matching perspective. Additionally, the reinforcement learning module is employed for adversarial training, executing de-obfuscation operations on payloads for which the CLCNN outputs a low confidence level, further processing ambiguous payloads. The experimental results indicate that even without a priori knowledge, CLCNN demonstrates strong resistance to such tools. And the RL module can further enhance Recall through adversarial training, with minimal reduction in the hybrid model's performance on standard SQLi datasets.

title: Physics-Informed iTransformer for Large-Capacity Lithium-ion Batteries Temperature Prediction under Alternating Current Preheating with Few Initial Cycles
authors: Tao Zhang, Mingwei Gao, Qiqi Jia, Zhengxiang Song, Jinhao Meng
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Industrial Electronics
abstract: Alternating current preheating (ACP) serves as an effective solution for enhancing the performance of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) during low-temperature operation. Accurately forecasting the temperature variation during ACP can facilitate the heating process and provide sufficient safety margins for battery thermal management. This paper proposes a physics informed iTransformer model for predicting LIB temperature under ACP, which is capable of forecasting the temperature variation of any unknown ACP process using only six initial cycles. To achieve high accuracy within few cycles, the physical mechanisms is integrated to the iTransformer during pre-training, while six initial cycles are utilized for transfer learning. Furthermore, a special designed data reconstruction method is proposed to enable temperature prediction for any future cycle with a single fixed input. Experimental validation is conducted using two groups of square batteries under ACP test at -17°C and 23°C. Results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves mean absolute errors (MAEs) of 0.1939 and 0.0785 at -17°C and 23°C, respectively.

title: Adaptive Fuzzy Fixed-Time Tracking Control for a Robotic Manipulator: A Novel Global Prescribed Performance Function Method
authors: Jihang Sui, Ben Niu, Luyao Wen, Yongsheng Ou
abstract: This paper researches the adaptive fixed-time global prescribed performance tracking control (GPPTC) problem for a robotic manipulator (RM) under unknown backlash-like hysteresis and disturbance. In the control design of the RM, a novel global fixed-time prescribed performance function (FTPPF) is designed to achieve output tracking with arbitrarily prescribed transient performance. Moreover, fuzzy logic systems (FLSs) are used to handle the unknown robotic dynamics, and a hyperbolic tangent activation function is applied to deal with the hysteresis nonlinearity. The proposed prescribed performance control (PPC) method based on the FTPPF ensures that the tracking error converges to a predetermined range in a given time and remains within that range thereafter. Unlike the existing PPC methods, our control algorithm removes the limitation requiring the initial tracking error to satisfy the initial value of the PPF. Based on the fixed-time command-filtered (FTCF) technique, an adaptive fixed-time controller is designed, which enables the joint position to track the desired signal in a fixed time. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed controller is demonstrated via the simulation.

title: Classification and Diagnosis of Propeller Damages Using FFT-Based Motor Current Signature Analysis of DC-Link Current
authors: Giho Lee, Sewoong Park, Seungmin Choi, Sanga Lee, Jinseok Jeong, Donguk Lee
abstract: This study proposes a frequency-domain diagnostic approach for detecting and characterizing unmanned aerial vehicle(UAV) propeller damage using only motor electrical signals. Motor current signals were analyzed via fast Fourier transform (FFT) across 36 systematically defined damage scenarios covering multiple locations and severity levels. The results demonstrate that specific harmonic components of the DC-link current exhibit strong and consistent sensitivity to propeller damage, with the sixth harmonic showing the most pronounced deviation from the normal state. Quantitative analysis revealed clear location-dependent trends: leading-edge damage produced frequency standard deviations up to 93.5 Hz, approximately 2.26 times higher than trailing-edge damage, while tip damage caused progressive bandwidth expansion, reaching 322.07 Hz, the largest variability observed. In contrast, hub-proximal damage exhibited minimal deviation, with bandwidths as low as 108.7 Hz. These findings confirm that motor current signals alone enable reliable discrimination of propeller damage presence, type, and coarse location, providing a practical and scalable solution for sensor less UAV ground inspection and maintenance.

title: Super-Resolution Approach for Low-Cost and Low-Resolution Radars
authors: Netanel Blumenfeld, Inna Stainvas, Igal Bilik
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Low-cost radars have emerged as attractive alternatives to optical sensors for consumer applications due to their robustness and inherent privacy. However, their limited spatial resolution constrains their applicability to tasks requiring fine motion analysis. This work proposes a deep learning-based super-resolution (SR) framework that enhances radar range- Doppler maps with task-specific fidelity. The proposed approach integrates a spatially adaptive feature modulation network and introduces classification-guided training that selectively refines gesture-relevant features. Unlike conventional image SR techniques focused on perceptual quality, the proposed approach jointly optimizes SR and classification objectives to impr ve radar-based hand gesture recognition (HGR). Three scalable architectures, SuperGestNet, MultiSuperGestNet, and RecursiveSuperGestNet, are proposed, offering trade-offs between accuracy, computational complexity, and memory. Experiments on real radar data demonstrate substantial improvements in HGR accuracy over conventional SR methods and baseline classifiers, even surpassing high-resolution models in some settings. This taskaware SR paradigm enables the practical deployment of low-cost, low-resolution radars for various consumer applications.

title: Covert Communications in MEC-Based Networked ISAC Systems Towards Low-Altitude Economy
authors: Weihao Mao, Yang Lu, Bo Ai, Tony Q. S. Quek
publish: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Communications
abstract: Low-altitude economy (LAE) is an emerging business model, which heavily relies on integrated sensing and communications (ISAC), mobile edge computing (MEC), and covert communications. This paper investigates the covert transmission design in MEC-based networked ISAC systems towards LAE, where an MEC server coordinates multiple access points to simultaneously receive computation tasks from multiple unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), locate a target in a sensing area, and maintain the UAVs’ covert transmission against multiple wardens. We first derive closed-form expressions for the detection error probability (DEP) at the wardens. Then, we formulate a total energy consumption minimization problem by optimizing communication, sensing, and computation resources as well as UAV trajectories, subject to the requirements on the quality of MEC services, DEP, and the radar signal-to-interference-and-noise ratio, and the causality constraints of UAV trajectories. An alternating optimization-based algorithm is proposed to handle the considered problem, which decomposes it into two subproblems: joint optimization of communication, sensing, and computation resources, and UAV trajectory optimization. The former is addressed by a successive convex approximation-based algorithm, while the latter is solved via a trust-region-based algorithm. Simulations validate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm compared with various benchmarks, and reveal the trade-offs among communication, sensing, and computation in LAE systems.

title: Proton Radiation Effects on Mode-Locked Operation of Three-Section AlGaInAs/InP Lasers for Space Applications
authors: Di Huang, Colin L. Bryant, Jiabao Li, En Xia Zhang, Michael W. McCurdy, Robert A. Reed, Peter J. Delfyett
abstract: We report, for the first time, an experimental investigation of proton radiation-induced effects on a three-section AlGaInAs/InP mode-locked laser diode (MLLD), using 1.8 MeV protons at a fluence of 1 × 10¹² /cm². Post-irradiation characterization shows stable passive mode-locking operation with improved overall performance. The threshold current increased and slope efficiency decreased due to defect-induced nonradiative recombination and absorption, yet the pulse width remained nearly unchanged at 1.44 ps with an improved time–bandwidth product of 0.438. Optical spectra exhibited a redshift, linewidth broadening, and better optical frequency comb tooth coherency. Fixed-point analysis demonstrated altered dispersion characteristics and enhanced tunability, indicating potential benefits for drift compensation and Doppler shift management in intra-satellite optical links. Multi-tone optical injection locking further confirmed robust post-irradiation performance, with stronger coherency transfer, improved RF sideband suppression (13.9 dB to 34.6 dB), narrower pulses (1.60 ps to 1.44 ps), and reduced time–bandwidth products, albeit requiring slightly higher injection power. These results indicate that this specific three-section MLLDs maintains functionality after proton exposure and exhibits features advantageous for compact, high-coherence comb generation in satellite and deep-space optical communication systems.

title: Bridging Extreme Viewpoint Gap: Robust Cross-Domain Matching of Tower Surveillance and Satellite Images for Precise Geolocation
authors: Pu Chen, Yuxuan Liu, Li Zhang, Zhihua Hu, Linjun Lu, Svetlana Besklubova, Xueping Zhang, Ioannis Brilakis
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Tower-based surveillance (TBS) systems provide continuous, high-resolution, and real-time monitoring over wide spatial extents. However, accurate geolocation of TBS imagery remains largely underexplored due to large viewpoint discrepancies, nonlinear radiometric distortions, and limited spatial overlap with satellite imagery. To address these challenges, we propose TowerSatLoc, a high-precision and fully automated correspondence-based geolocation framework that registers TBS imagery to geo-referenced satellite images under complex conditions. The framework first applies a Bird’s Eye View transformation using intrinsic and extrinsic camera parameters to reduce viewpoint discrepancies. Within this process, an adaptive projection factor and an automatic orientation estimation method are introduced to improve geometric consistency. To handle severe cross-domain differences, semantic representations extracted from DINOv2 are fused with multi-scale geometric features from VGG19 and refined through hierarchical residual matching and confidence-aware resampling. The framework further incorporates robustness-enhancing mechanisms to improve localization reliability under extreme viewpoint variations and uncertain tower geocoordinates. Extensive experiments on a newly constructed dataset, TowSatData, demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of TowerSatLoc across diverse real-world scenarios, providing a scalable and generalizable solution for correspondence-driven cross-view geolocation.

title: Outlier-Aware Contrastive Learning
authors: Jen-Tzung Chien, Kuan Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Contrastive learning aims to learn an embedding space with sample discrimination where similar samples attract together while dissimilar samples repulse apart. However, the issue of sampling bias likely happens and degrades the classification performance when a contrast model is trained with the leakage caused by similar samples but from different classes or dissimilar samples from the same class. Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection provides a meaningful scheme to detect and mask those false negative samples for debiasing in an outlier-aware contrastive loss for high-fidelity contrastive learning. Sample debiasing is feasible to reduce the upper bound of contrastive loss. Also, the previous OOD detector was trained from auxiliary collection of OOD samples. In real world, the prior knowledge of OOD samples is commonly unavailable. This study presents new outlier-aware detection and contrast models through generation and augmentation of those samples near the boundary between in-distribution (ID) and OOD. These synthesized samples are located right outside ID, and their Gaussian embeddings sufficiently reflect OOD behaviors. An OOD detector is learned by using ID samples and synthesized OOD samples with the learning objective towards contrastive OOD detection and debiased contrast model. The experiments are conducted to illustrate the merit of the proposed outlier-aware contrastive learning.

title: Multi-Expert Genetic Programming based Ensemble for Long-Tailed Image Classification_supp1-3669207.pdf
authors: Ruwang Jiao
abstract: Long-tailed image classification faces challenges of data imbalance and poor feature representation for tail classes, leading to biased predictions favoring head classes. While most existing methods rely on deep neural networks (DNNs), they typically require large amounts of training data and lack interpretability. Genetic Programming (GP) has shown promise in few-shot learning but has seldom been investigated in long-tailed image classification, primarily due to its limited ability to handle class imbalance and its tendency for fitness functions to be biased toward head classes. To fill this gap, this paper proposes a multi-expert GP method for long-tailed image classification.We develop three objective functions, each serving as an expert: 1) a longtailed expert focusing on head-class performance; 2) a balanced-class expert that promotes equal class representation; and 3) an inverse long-tailed expert emphasizing tail classes. This triexpert framework enables GP to jointly optimize complementary objectives and learn robust feature representations for both head and tail classes. To further improve classification performance, the evolved GP individuals from the final population are used to train base learners, and their outputs are integrated via a voting-based ensemble model. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art GP and DNN approaches without pretraining across seven long-tailed image classification datasets.

title: Two-Way Relay Systems: Evaluating the Impact of Pre-Equalization and Constellation Design
authors: Saud Althunibat, Raed Mesleh
abstract: This paper proposes a novel two-way relaying (TWR) with optimized precoding at the source nodes during the first time slot. Relaying techniques are widely considered to extend the connectivity between wireless nodes when direct transmission becomes unreliable or infeasible due to long distances or obstructed propagation paths. Conventional relaying techniques include amplify-and-forward (AF) and decode-and-forward (DF) systems. TWR enhances the spectral efficiency of conventional techniques by allowing bidirectional communication in only two time slots. In the proposed TWR method, optimized precoding is applied at the source nodes during the first time slot, while conventional TWR transmission is maintained during the second time slot. Theoretical analysis and Monte Carlo simulation results demonstrate notable performance gains in AF systems. Yet, a consistent 3 dB improvement is reported in DF configurations. The impact of constellation phase rotation on the end-to-end performance is also examined. It is shown that in DF systems, phase rotation improves first-hop detection; while its influence on the end-to-end performance remains limited due to the second hop bottleneck. Avergae bit error rate (BER) results under both perfect and imperfect channel estimation confirm that the proposed pre-equalization outperforms conventional TWR methods across a range of modulation orders.

title: When Movable Antenna Meets Downlink NOMA: Transmit Beamforming and Discrete Antenna Position Optimization
authors: Ziyi Jia, Zheng Xiang, Long Yang, Lu Lv, Arumugam Nallanathan
abstract: This paper investigates the joint optimization of transmit beamforming and discrete antenna positions for maximally improving the sum rate of movable antenna (MA) enhanced downlink non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) systems. As such an optimization problem belongs to a challenging mixed-integer nonlinear programming, we first develop an equivalent problem reformulation, with which we further propose a two-sided matching assisted optimization algorithm to jointly optimize the discrete antenna positions and transmit beamforming. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that the proposed algorithm always converges to a stable matching within polynomial complexity. Simulation results show that NOMA-MA using our proposed algorithm not only significantly outperforms the NOMA-MA using sequential optimization and the reconfigurable intelligent surface aided fixed position antenna (FPA) empowered NOMA, but also achieves a better performance gain over FPA than its orthogonal multiple access counterpart.

title: Robust Joint Design for Integrated Communication and Over-the-Air Computation in Cooperative Cognitive Relay Networks
authors: Qishuo Wang, Changjie Hu, Quanzhong Li, Liang Yang
abstract: The rapid growth of wireless devices for communication and computation in wireless networks makes spectrum scarcity an increasingly prominent challenge. To alleviate this issue, we investigate a cooperative cognitive relay network, which consists of a primary network aiming at communication and a secondary network assisted by multiple cognitive relays responsible for over-the-air computation (AirComp), where the secondary network helps relay the signals for the primary network in exchange for opportunity of using the spectrum of the primary network, to transmit its own information. Considering the scenario where only imperfect channel state information (CSI) is available, we try to minimize the worst-case computation mean square error of the AirComp network while ensuring the quality-of-service of the primary network, by jointly designing the transmit coefficients of the sensors, the amplify-and-forward coefficients of the relays and the beamforming vector of the access point. The formulated robust optimization problem is challenging due to the imperfect CSI and highly-coupled variables. By employing the cutting-set (CS) method and the concave-convex constrained procedure (CCCP), we propose an effective CS-CCCP algorithm to solve the robust problem. Numerical results demonstrate the superiority of our proposed scheme over the baseline methods.

title: Large Kernel Information Distillation Network for super-resolution
authors: Yi Wu, Yujie Wang, Kaige Cui, Qiaoqi Zhang
publish: 2025 7th International Conference on Intelligent Control, Measurement and Signal Processing (ICMSP)
abstract: Recent advances in deep convolutional neural networks have significantly boosted the performance of single image super-resolution (SISR). Nevertheless, the high computational cost, large number of parameters, and long inference time of most existing models pose major challenges for deployment on resource-constrained platforms. To address the aforementioned challenges, this paper proposes a lightweight network named Large Kernel Information Distillation Network (LKIDN). First, the proposed model incorporates multi-scale receptive field integration, which enables the network to capture richer pixel dependencies and fuse hierarchical contextual information during the feature extraction stage. Second, we introduce a pair of efficient attention modules, namely ECCA and SSA, designed to enhance the reconstruction quality of super-resolved images with only a small increase in parameter count. Extensive experiments are conducted on standard benchmark datasets (Set5 and Set14) for ×2, ×3, and ×4 super-resolution tasks. Compared with the baseline model, our method achieves an improvement of more than 0.15 dB in PSNR, while maintaining a significantly smaller number of parameters, demonstrating its superior balance between reconstruction accuracy and model efficiency.

title: An Intelligent Live Cable Identification Method Based on Multi-Layer Anti-Interference Strategy
authors: Jiale Zhang, Yan Fang, Jin Gu
publish: 2025 6th International Symposium on New Energy and Electrical Technology (ISNEET)
abstract: To address the decline in identification accuracy caused by power-frequency noise, electromagnetic coupling, and environmental interference during live cable detection, this paper proposes an intelligent cable identification method based on a multi-layer adaptive anti-interference strategy. The method integrates wavelet threshold denoising and adaptive LMS filtering to jointly suppress random noise and narrowband interference. Three categories of features—amplitude, phase consistency, and narrowband signal-to-noise ratio—are extracted and fused through a weighted decision model to achieve high-precision identification. Simulation results show that, under an input SNR of 10–20 dB, the proposed method improves the output SNR by approximately 1.6 dB and achieves an identification accuracy exceeding 95%. The proposed approach features lightweight computation, real-time performance, and strong robustness, providing an effective solution for non-interrupted cable identification in power distribution systems.

title: The SpectrumX Mobile Experiment Platform
authors: Nicholas Rainville, John Marino, Scott Palo, Frank Lind, Ryan Volz, Greta Fergus, Chris Eckert, Aleksandar PopStefanija, Chris McKenney, Randy Herban, J. Nicholas Laneman
abstract: The SpectrumX Mobile Experiment Platform (MEP) is an software radio instrument designed to support a wide range of spectrum experiments. It is capable of passive data acquisition as well as active transmission to enable research into signal propagation and waveform co-existence. The mobile instrument includes an analog front-end with calibration, positioning, and timing features, a field-programmable gate array (FPGA) radio, and an Artificial Intelligence (AI) compute platform for on-instrument data processing. Four synchronous receive channels and two transmit channels operate up to 6 GHz, with an optional wide-band tuner. The hardware, firmware, and software for the MEP are extensible and reproducible, with components available through a combination of off-the-shelf hardware and rapid prototyping fabrication. More than a dozen MEPs have been constructed through student workshops and are being used for ongoing field experiments. This paper details the design of the MEP, including component selection and initial testing.

title: ICMSP 2025 Preface
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 7th International Conference on Intelligent Control, Measurement and Signal Processing (ICMSP)

title: A Multi-Voltage-Level Harmonic Tracing Method Based on Correlation Analysisle
authors: Wenxiao Ma, Dandan Feng, Tongxun Wang, Yang Wang
publish: 2025 6th International Symposium on New Energy and Electrical Technology (ISNEET)
abstract: Harmonic exceeding-limit has become a critical power-quality issue that must be urgently solved; accurately locating the harmonic source is the prerequisite for effective mitigation. Although various harmonic-tracing methods have been proposed, their on-site, case-specific application remains to be realized. This paper presents a harmonic-tracing approach that combines correlation analysis with phasor projection. By calculating system impedance and quantifying the harmonic-responsibility contribution of both the utility and customer sides, the exact location of the harmonic source can be determined. Field tests on a 10 kV feeder and a 400 V distribution area in Henan Province confirm that the proposed method satisfies tracing accuracy requirements and offers a feasible scheme for promoting on-site harmonic tracing.

title: Route Choice Prediction Through User Behavior Analysis: Towards Robustness Assessment Under External Perturbations
authors: Gustavo Sánchez, Fatih Ünal, Alexandra Wins
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Trust, Privacy and Security in Intelligent Systems, and Applications (TPS-ISA)
abstract: We present a preliminary study of route choice prediction and its robustness to small, realistic perturbations, with emphasis on attack surfaces that matter for transport and energy infrastructure. Using a synthetic, richly annotated route dataset, we train per-user Random Forest models (single-model test accuracy 82 %) and aggregate explanations via model-specific Gini importances and Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations (LIME) attributions. Feature analysis shows that distance and duration dominate the global Gini ranking while LIME highlights temporal indicators as highly explanatory at the instance level. We probe sensitivity with nearest-neighbor counterfactuals and find that modest edits-e.g., sub-kilometre distance shifts <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(0.4-1.2 ~\text{km})$</tex> or small hierarchy changes-can flip predicted choices; an ensemble majority vote over ten user models generalizes to a held-out user with 80 % accuracy. Building on these findings, we formalize a problem-space threat model, discuss the inverse feature-mapping challenge that an attacker must solve to translate feature goals into map/sensor edits, and consider realistic vectors such as fake charger listings and traffic spoofing. We conclude with mitigation directions and a discussion of regulatory implications under the EU AI Act, and outline next steps including in-vehicle validation.

title: Real-Time Galloping Monitoring of Overhead Transmission Lines Based on Spatiotemporal Electromagnetic Coupling of Ground Wires
authors: Weigang Zheng, Hong Tang, Yuxiao Zhou, Shicong Sun, Jiayu Han
publish: 2025 6th International Symposium on New Energy and Electrical Technology (ISNEET)
abstract: Overhead transmission lines are prone to galloping under strong wind, icing, and aerodynamic disturbances, which may lead to phase-to-phase faults, line trips, or even conductor breakage. Existing galloping monitoring technologies rely on accelerometers, gyroscopes, or video systems, yet they often suffer from high installation complexity, high cost, and poor resistance to electromagnetic interference. Since the motion of ground wires alters the surrounding spatiotemporal electromagnetic field distribution, a passive sensing approach based on electromagnetic coupling provides a promising new direction for real-time galloping monitoring. This paper proposes a real-time galloping sensing technique for overhead lines based on the spatiotemporal electromagnetic coupling mechanism between conductors and ground wires. A mathematical mapping is established among conductor motion, electromagnetic disturbance, and sensing signals. High-sensitivity magnetoelectric sensing nodes are designed to perform multi-point spatiotemporal synchronous monitoring. Furthermore, a spatiotemporal feature–fusion galloping identification algorithm is developed to achieve high-accuracy estimation of galloping amplitude, frequency, and phase. Simulation and field test results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves millimeter-level displacement detection under strong electromagnetic interference, offering a new technical pathway for intelligent sensing in modern power grids.

title: Resolution Analysis of the S-Transform with a Modified Gaussian Window
authors: Jiaqi Wang, Wei Wang, Jun Zhang
publish: 2025 7th International Conference on Intelligent Control, Measurement and Signal Processing (ICMSP)
abstract: Traditional time-frequency analysis methods often fall short in analyzing non-stationary signals characterized by wide frequency spans and complex variations. To slove this problem, this paper advances a modified S-transform algorithm based on an optimized gaussian window function. We achieve enhanced time-frequency resolution by controlling the shape of the window function through reconfiguring the scaling factor of the gaussian window. This adaptation allows the algorithm to tailor its time-frequency analysis to the characteristics of different signals, thereby preventing the window from becoming excessively narrow at low frequencies or overly broad at high frequencies. As a result, a balanced resolution is maintained across the entire time-frequency plane, significantly improving the overall energy concentration. Finally, simulation experiments verify the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm and its applicability to various types of signals.

title: SAFE (Smart Alert Framework for Elephant) - Ai Based Human-Wildlife Conflict Prevention Using Yolo: A Review and Analysis
authors: F. Sofiah, D. Sivaganesan, N. Suba Rani, A.Noble Mary Juliet, N. Senthil Madasamy, J. Bhavithra
publish: 2025 IEEE First International Conference on Innovations in Engineering and Next-Generation Technologies for Sustainability (ICINVENTS)
abstract: Human-Elephant conflict is a critical issue in wildlife conservation and rural agriculture, particularly in regions with dense elephant populations. Conventional monitoring methods such as manual patrolling and camera traps lack real-time capabilities and often fail to prevent damage and loss. This paper presents a comprehensive review of existing AI-based wildlife monitoring techniques, with a focus on YOLO (You Only Look Once) models, edge computing, and IoT-based alert systems. The study compares 20 recent research papers highlighting advances in real-time animal detection, emotion analysis, and low-cost scalable solutions for rural areas. Our analysis identifies gaps in behavioral prediction and edge-device deployment, leading to a proposal for a Smart Alert Framework for Elephants (SAFE) that ensures early detection, aggression prediction, and realtime alerts to farmers and forest officers

title: Modeling and Simulation of Three-phase Photovoltaic Grid-connected Inverter
authors: Jiandong Jin, Chengwen Zhou, Kaiyan Wang, Qingsen Cai, Xizhe Li, Li Ma, Jiakun Wang
abstract: With the development of the renewable energy industry, photovoltaic grid-connected power generation systems have been widely applied. The isolated photovoltaic grid-connected inverter with a transformer can isolate DC components on the one hand, and improve the safety of the system on the other. Although the mathematical model of a three-phase grid-connected inverter in a three-phase symmetrical stationary coordinate system possesses the characteristic of clear physical significance, the control variables of the mathematical model in this coordinate system are time-varying AC quantities, which is not conducive to the design of a controller. Through coordinate rotation transformation, the conversion of control variables from AC to DC can be achieved, making the design of regulators for DC control systems much simpler. This paper establishes a mathematical model for a three-phase photovoltaic grid-connected inverter and designs a control system. It provides a detailed introduction to mathematical modeling, decoupling, and the design of the regulator. Finally, the design of the regulator is verified through simulation, and the closed-loop design meets the system stability conditions and enables the system to operate stably and rapidly.

title: Explainable AI in Data Poisoning Threat Models Across the CIA Triad: A Smart Grid Case Study
authors: Gustavo Sánchez, Ghada Elbez, Veit Hagenmeyer
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Trust, Privacy and Security in Intelligent Systems, and Applications (TPS-ISA)
abstract: eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) techniques make models more interpretable, but this can help malicious actors to better achieve their goals. In this paper, we systematically apply data poisoning to the most important features according to distinct XAI methods, and evaluate its impact on learning-based model architectures trained on realistic Smart Grid (SG) datasets. Our approach involves poisoning top-ranked features to determine which XAI method-or combination thereof-provides the most actionable insights to improve data poisoning attacks in different settings. Furthermore, we perform a practical problemspace attack via Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) time spoofing, where time-related features identified as most critical by XAI techniques are subsequently exploited, leading to a decrease in Intrusion Detection System (IDS) performance. This work contributes to the community by offering new insights into the vulnerability of critical infrastructure systems to sophisticated -yet realistic- adversarial attacks.

title: Smart Energy System for Ferrous Metallurgical Industry: Energy Flow Modeling, Carbon Footprint Tracking, and Scheduling Optimization
authors: Xiaoxuan Mao
publish: 2025 6th International Symposium on New Energy and Electrical Technology (ISNEET)
abstract: The ferrous metallurgical sector is generally faced with the problem of energy use, work ability, and uncertainty of carbon emission limits, as well as poor energy dispatch, and therefore, it is somewhat complicated to balance the reduction of carbon emissions and economic gains at the same time. To discuss these problems, this paper develops an energy flow modeling system consisting of a combination of physical and data-driven modeling. The system provides a good representation of the relationship between the energy and material flow through collaborative modeling of energy flow and material flow, and represents the entire process, i.e. sintering to blast furnace to converter to rolling mill. A carbon footprint tracking model is put in place to assign energy consumption and carbon sources to every production unit, which results in quantitative carbon emissions and traceability of processes. Based on this, a multi-objective formulation of a scheduling optimization model is developed that aims at unit product carbon emissions, energy costs and system energy efficiency. An improved Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II) is used for optimization. The model is validated on data from a 5 million-ton-level steel enterprise, showing an energy flow model prediction error of less than 3.2%, and an overall deviation between predicted and measured carbon emissions controlled within ±2%. The results show that the method presented in this paper can achieve accurate characterization and coordinated scheduling of carbon flow in ferrous metallurgical processes, providing a feasible path for the low-carbon transformation of industry.

title: A Decentralized Framework for Auditing Large Language Model Reasoning
authors: Morris Yu-Chao Huang, Zhen Tan, Mohan Zhang, Pingzhi Li, Zezhen Ding, Zhuo Zhang, Tianlong Chen
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Trust, Privacy and Security in Intelligent Systems, and Applications (TPS-ISA)
abstract: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) represent a paradigm shift in AI capabilities, enabling autonomous multistep decision-making across critical domains. However, their opaque reasoning traces pose unprecedented risks in high-stakes applications like healthcare, criminal justice, and autonomous systems. Current centralized auditing approaches fail to provide the transparency, scalability, and trust necessary for safe deployment. We propose a decentralized blockchain-based auditing framework that enables verifiable reasoning validation without compromising proprietary model secrets.

title: Wide-Tuning, Low-Distortion CMOS Sine Wave Generator in 180nm CMOS Technology for Cryo-Cooler Drivers and DC–AC Conversion
authors: Nishant Kumar, Hari Shanker Gupta, Shruti Singh, Chandra Prakash Sharma, M. Senthil Kumar, Nihar Ranjan Mohapatra
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Emerging Electronics (ICEE)
abstract: This paper presents a fully analog sine wave generator implemented in 180nm CMOS technology, optimized for cryo-cooler compressor drivers. The circuit employs a dual-slope ramp generator followed by an analog pulse-shaping network, enabling continuous frequency tuning from 50mHz to 185kHz with Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) below 0.5%. It delivers a 360mV<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">pp</inf> output, operates robustly across process, voltage, and temperature variations, and requires only a single external capacitor for tuning. The compact, low-power architecture supports both sub-Hz excitation for thermal characterization and higher-frequency operation for system diagnostics & low-power cryo-cooler operation, making it a strong candidate for integration into space-borne cryo-cooler driver SoCs.

title: Research on Optimal Scheduling with Transmission-Distribution Coordination Considering Large-Scale Electric Vehicles
authors: Li Zhe, Wang Yidi, Li Yuxuan, Xu Xiao, Zhang Chuancheng, Cai Zhi
abstract: Based on large-scale integration of electric vehicles (EVs), this paper studies optimal scheduling of a transmission-distribution coordinated system using the Bitter Fish Optimization algorithm to provide efficient and economical scheduling solutions. A multi-objective coordination model is developed to overcome the limitations of existing and decoupled scheduling. The objective minimizes electricity procurement costs, including thermal generation costs, photovoltaic (PV) and wind generation costs of storing power on the transmission side, as well as EVs charging and discharging on the distribution side. Constraints include network operational limits, EVs charging and discharging, with wind and PV data obtained from forecasting and modal decomposition. A coordinated scheduling scenario with large-scale EVs is constructed, and a coordinated IEEE 30-node and 33-node test system with wind and PV is designed showing that peak-valley price outperforms fixed price in cost reduction, producing diversified and high-performance scheduling schemes for generators, EVs, and flexible loads, offering new insights for modern power system operation.

title:
RF-Sputtered BaTiO
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    Dielectrics for High-Mobility, Low-Voltage Organic Field-Effect Transistors
authors: Subharthi Saha, Shiv Prakash Verma, Samik Mallik, Sovanlal Mondal, Subhadip Mondal, Dipak Kumar Goswami
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Emerging Electronics (ICEE)
abstract: The realization of stable, low-voltage operated, and high-sensitive Organic Field-Effect Transistors (OFETs) is critical for advancing next-generation organic electronics. In this work, OFETs were fabricated using radio-frequency (RF) sputtered Barium Titanate (BaTiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf>) thin films as the gate dielectric. Unlike conventional sol-gel-based, spin-coated BaTiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> films, which often exhibit high surface roughness and significant leakage currents, sputtered BaTiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> provides superior film uniformity and reduced leakage owing to high-vacuum deposition in an oxygen–argon plasma environment. Pentacene was employed as the active semiconductor, and the resulting devices demonstrated lowered threshold voltages and enhanced charge transport with exceptional high field effect carrier mobility. Notably, a field-effect mobility of ~9 cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>/V•s was achieved at 100 kHz, with mobility increasing further at higher frequencies. In contrast, devices employing spin-coated BaTiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> exhibited nearly frequency-independent mobility, remaining around ~1 cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>/V•s even at higher frequencies. The approach is fully compatible with CMOS and advanced microfabrication techniques. These results establish RF-sputtered BaTiO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> as a scalable, high-performance dielectric for OFETs, enabling low-power operation and opening pathways for applications in logic circuits, biosensing, flexible electronics, gas detection, and neuromorphic systems, etc.

title: Leveraging Transformer Models and eXplainable Reinforcement Learning Methods for Advanced Intrusion Detection and Response System
authors: Mohammad Ghasemigol, Daniel Takabi
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Trust, Privacy and Security in Intelligent Systems, and Applications (TPS-ISA)
abstract: As cyber threats become increasingly sophisticated and frequent, the need for advanced Intrusion Detection and Response Systems (IDRS) is more critical than ever. These systems are essential for defending networks against external and internal intrusions by detecting potential threats and applying appropriate responses. However, a significant challenge faced by existing IDRSs is the overwhelming volume of alerts generated by IDSs, which makes manual response impractical. Additionally, the effectiveness of automated IRS is often undermined by difficulties in accurately estimating response costs, assessing the network situation, and providing clear explanations for chosen responses. These challenges often lead to suboptimal responses, negatively impacting network performance and pushing administrators towards inefficient manual methods. To overcome these challenges, this paper introduces a novel approach that integrates transformer encoder, decision transformer, and eXplainable Reinforcement Learning (XRL) methods to build an AI-powered IDRS. Experiments show that the proposed transformer-based IDS achieves performance on par with or surpass traditional treebased and neural models on tabular network flows. Despite similar performance, transformers offer greater flexibility and adaptability to shifting feature distributions and evolving attack patterns, making them well-suited for real-world deployment. I In addition, the proposed Decision Transformer-based IRS enables dynamic selection of optimal responses tailored to the current network situation, detected attack type, and contextual constraints, offering explainable and adaptive defense in real time.
