title: Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning for Energy-Aware Spectrum Management in 6G IoT Network
authors: Emmanuel M. Migabo
publish: 2025 5th International Multidisciplinary Information Technology and Engineering Conference (IMITEC)
abstract: This paper proposes a reinforcement learning-based framework for intelligent spectrum access in a 6G-enabled Internet of Things (IoT) network. The environment consists of multiple heterogeneous agents, modelling IoT devices with varying energy budgets, traffic patterns, and communication demands. The agents must share a limited set of wireless channels efficiently, using low energy, without collisions, and with low latency. The problem is formulated as a decentralised partially observable Markov decision process (Dec-POMDP) and solved using Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimisation (MAPPO). MAPPO enables each agent to learn an energy- and delay-aware transmission policy through centralized training with decentralized execution. Each agent observes its local environment and makes decisions independently, while policy learning is guided using global state information through a centralized critic. Simulation results demonstrate that, compared with a random-access baseline, the proposed MAPPO framework improves transmission success rate by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 85 \%$</tex>, reduces collisions by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>50 \%$</tex>, and lowers average buffer (delay proxy) by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\approx 30 \%$</tex> in our setting (10 agents, 2 channels, 800 episodes). The results confirm that reinforcement learning provides an effective solution for adaptive and scalable spectrum management in future 6G IoT networks.

title: An Efficient FPGA Implementation of Spiking Neural Networks for N-MNIST Classification
authors: Adhish JS, Vijaya Kumar K, Suresh Balanethiram
publish: 2025 IEEE 7th International Conference on Emerging Electronics (ICEE)
abstract: The demand for low-power artificial intelligence on edge devices has spurred research into brain-inspired computing paradigms like Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs). While SNNs promise significant energy savings, their implementation on resource-constrained hardware like FPGAs presents a considerable challenge for edge applications. This paper details a complete hardware-software co-design workflow for deploying an SNN for handwritten digit classification. A hardware-aware, three-layer SNN is trained offline in Python using a surrogate gradient method, and the learned weights are deployed for inference on a Xilinx Artix-7 FPGA. The architecture features a resource-efficient, multiplier-less digital Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) neuron and a time-multiplexed dataflow to minimize hardware utilization. Implemented on a Basys 3 board, our design achieves 96.5% classification accuracy on the MNIST dataset, consuming only 4,808 Look-Up Tables (LUTs) and 2 DSP slices. These results demonstrate a highly competitive balance between accuracy and resource efficiency, validating the design’s suitability for low-power edge applications.

title: 36.4 ANP-OT: A 0.17nW/Synapse 0.46pJ/SOP Neuromorphic Olfactory Processor with On-Chip Transfer Learning for Non-Invasive Cross-Hospital Cross-Pulmonary-Disease Diagnosis
authors: Dexuan Huo, Ziyi Cheng, Jilin Zhang, Yumeng Jiang, Lushuo Zhang, Hui Wang, Na Ma, Zebin Huang, Minggui Lin, Yunxia Zhao, Zhihua Wang, Kea-Tiong Tang, Hong Chen
abstract: This paper reports an olfactory processor with on-chip transfer learning for cross-hospital and cross-pulmonary-disease diagnosis, which achieves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>94 \%$</tex> pulmonary disease detection accuracy at 3 different hospitals. This work achieves a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.46 \text{pJ} /$</tex> SOP energy efficiency, and a power density of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.17 \text{nW} /$</tex> synapse.

title: Frequency-Domain Modeling and Integrated Power Decoupling of the Cross-Coupled Powers in MAB Converters Using a Full-Order Impedance Matrix
authors: Ayooluwa Ajiboye, Chanaka Singhabahu, Benjamin Shani, Ayodhya Somiruwan Gamwari, Alireza Khaligh
abstract: The multi-active bridge (MAB) converter has gained widespread adoption due to its multidirectional power flow via a galvanically isolated multi-winding magnetic structure. However, the mutually-shared magnetic link and inter-port phase-shifts induce the cross-coupled power flow which hinders independent port-wise operation. This work presents an analytical framework that combines port-equivalent models derived from a full-order <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$n \times n$</tex-math></inline-formula> impedance matrix (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bf {Z}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-matrix) with generalized harmonic analysis (GHA), to accurately capture the cross-coupled power flow in MAB converters. An experimental method based on steady-state tests is developed to characterize the cross-coupled active powers by isolating the inter-port power interactions. Thereafter, a control-integrated power decoupling (iPD) algorithm with feedforward compensation is developed to achieve port-wise decoupling by suppressing voltage fluctuations during transients. The required port-equivalent model parameters are obtained from the experimentally extracted <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bf {Z}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-matrix of the high-frequency magnetic link (HFML) via a proposed single-port impedance measurement scheme. The cross-coupling and decoupling frameworks are validated on a 2.4 kW SiC-based four-port MAB prototype. Transient experiments featuring both voltage- and load-step variations are used to verify the effectiveness and improved decoupling performance of the proposed <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\bf {Z}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-matrix iPD strategy, relative to the uncompensated PI controller baseline, and the reduced-order <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$Y$</tex-math></inline-formula>-<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\Delta$</tex-math></inline-formula> (star-delta) feedforward scheme.

title:
Highly-Sensitive Thermally-Stabilized Palladium Cantilever Nanogap H
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Sensor
authors: Amirali Nikeghbal, Md. Rabiul Hasan, Farhan Sadik Sium, Fatemeh Momeni, Adwait Deshpande, Seungbeom Noh, Hanseup Kim, Carlos H. Mastrangelo
abstract: We present a new palladium-based nanogap hydrogen sensor designed for fast, low-concentration H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> detection in environmental monitoring applications. The device consists of a long electrically-conductive low-stress palladium cantilever beam separated by a nanometer-sized air gap from a second cantilever beam of different length placed near the first beam tip thus forming a H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>-dependent capacitor. The cantilevers are anchored on top of a thermally-isolated SiN membrane with an integrated microheater that maintains the sensor at constant temperature. When exposed to H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>, the capacitor nanogap is reduced resulting in a change in capacitance with H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> sensitivity <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$S_{\mathrm {H2}} =0.15$</tex-math> </inline-formula> fF/ppm. The sensor shows a consistent response up to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$55~^{\circ }$</tex-math> </inline-formula>C with a thermal drift smaller than 0.22 aF/<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">o</sup>C and a minimum detectable signal of 10 ppm. The device repeatability was confirmed across 5 consecutive test cycles. Selectivity tests show negligible response to common interfering gases CO, CO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>, CH<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sub>, water vapor and NH<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$S_{i}\lt 5.2\times 1{0}^{-6}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>fF/ppm). The sensor exhibited a response time of approximately 390 ms upon exposure to H<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>.[2025-0141]

title: Proposal of General Fragility Curve Variables for Korean Transmission Towers on Wind Load
authors: Jonghun Woo, Jaewook Jung, Shinyoung Kwag, Seunghyun Eem
abstract: With the increasing frequency and intensity of extreme weather events driven by climate change, damage to power grids caused by typhoons and hurricanes accompanied by strong winds has been escalating worldwide. Consequently, assessing the wind-resistant safety of transmission towers, which are key components of the power grid, has become increasingly important. However, conducting fragility analysis for individual towers across an entire power grid presents substantial practical challenges due to significant time and cost constraints. To overcome this limitation, this study proposes fragility curve parameters that can be broadly applied to various transmission tower configurations and employed in power grid safety assessments. For this purpose, nonlinear static pushover analyses were performed on transmission tower models representing the standard 154 kV, 345 kV, and 765 kV configurations used in Korea, and the corresponding load–displacement capacity curves were derived. Based on these results, the median failure wind speed (Vm) and logarithmic standard deviation (β) were calculated for each voltage class and for an integrated model. These values were then used to develop generally applicable fragility curve parameters for transmission towers subjected to strong winds. The proposed parameters are expected to serve as an efficient and reliable tool for probabilistic safety evaluations of power grids and risk assessments under extreme wind events.

title: MELF-XFD: Trustworthy and Interpretable Multi-Expert Fusion for Safety-Critical Fault Diagnosis in Autonomous Underwater Vehicles
authors: Misha Urooj Khan, Ahmad Suleman, Yazeed Alkhrijah, Zeeshan Kaleem
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Vehicular Technology
abstract: Autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) operating in harsh, communication-limited underwater environments require robust fault diagnosis systems to ensure mission success and safety. Existing model-based approaches suffer from hydrodynamic modeling inaccuracies, while data-driven methods typically employ monolithic architectures that lack interpretability and real-time deployment awareness. This paper proposes Multi-Expert Lightweight Fusion Model with Ethical & Explainable Fault Diagnosis (MELF-XFD), a novel model-free framework that addresses these limitations through physically-grounded multi-domain signal decomposition. MELF-XFD performs explainable fault diagnosis by fusing temporal, spectral, and statistical representations of multivariate AUV time-series data using lightweight expert networks with adaptive weighting for real-time onboard deployment. Experimental results on a public AUV benchmark with five fault classes show that MELF-XFD outperforms existing methods, achieving a 95.7% macro-F1 score, 90.0% severe fault recall, and 39 ms inference latency. It attains the highest composite diagnostic criterion around 93.6%, balancing accuracy, safety-critical sensitivity, and computational efficiency. A low expected calibration error of 0.0880 ensures reliable confidence estimates for safety-critical deployment. Ablation studies confirm the critical role of temporal and frequency experts, while adaptive fusion enables interpretable fault attribution, establishing MELF-XFD as a practical and deployment-ready solution.

title: A Social Navigation Framework based on Laplace Group Dynamics for Humanoid Robot in the Dynamic Scenario
authors: Xuan Mu, Xiaorui Liu, Qingyang Fan, Lin Liu, Jinpeng Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Nowadays, humanoid robots are expected to possess perception and interaction abilities that can be applied in social applications. In this paper, efforts are made to enable a humanoid robot to identify potentially interactive human groups while avoiding pedestrians in dynamic scenarios. To this end, an integrated framework is proposed. In this framework, a group identification module is first developed to classify individuals into groups and effectively infer the group behavioral trend by monitoring their temporal characteristics. Then, an interactive decision maker based on Laplacian dynamics is established to guide the humanoid robot in making interactive decisions to ensure the target groups. Based on the above perception and decision designs, a novel robot behavior planning algorithm is designed to both approach interactive targets in a socially compliant manner and avoid non-interactive ones. Simulation and experiments based on a humanoid robot have demonstrated that the framework can achieve higher social planning speed and better interaction quality while maintaining satisfactory avoidance performance. These findings indicate that the Laplacian-based decision-maker can enable humanoid robots to efficiently respond to groups with various behavior patterns.

title: Design and Analysis of a Compact Triple-Band Fractal Antenna with Parasitic Elements and Meandered Transmission Line with Stub for IoT Applications
authors: Nihal Singh, Ashok Kumar, Anurag Vidyarthi, Arjun Kumar, Jitendra Kumar Deegwal, Abhishek Kandwal
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer, Internet of Things and Smart City (CIoTSC)
abstract: This paper demonstrates the design and extensive analysis of a novel, space-efficient compact triple-band fractal antenna with parasitic elements designed for Internet of Things (IoT) applications operating at 3.488 GHz, 4.092 GHz, and 5.036 GHz frequencies with peak return loss values of -34.41 dB, - 28.88 dB, and -21.20 dB, respectively. The antenna features a unique Minkowski fractal geometry for size reduction, a triple-band design while maintaining excellent impedance matching characteristics, and a meandered parasitic patch to enhance performance. A U-shaped microstrip impedance matching network with an open-circuit stub is employed to couple the feed line to the main antenna patch. The parasitic patch elements enhance gain and bandwidth performance across all operating frequency bands. Extensive parametric analysis was conducted using CST Microwave Studio to optimize transmission line lengths, stub positions, and meandered line configurations. The presented antenna demonstrates superior performance with VSWR values of 1.04, 1.07, and 1.19 at the respective resonant frequencies, making it highly suitable for smart city and IoT device integration.

title: Remote Sensing Monitoring and Spatiotemporal Analysis of Cultivated Land Occupied by Construction
authors: You Zhou, Zhugeng Duan, Liuxu Zeng
publish: 2025 International Conference on Computer, Internet of Things and Smart City (CIoTSC)
abstract: China's rapid urbanization has accelerated Farmland conversion to construction uses, threatening food security. This study assessed cultivated land loss in Jingbian County (2005-2020) using land-use data and high-resolution remote sensing, comparing three classification methods.The Decision Tree method achieved superior performance (>85% accuracy), significantly outperforming Support Vector Machine (>80%) and Maximum Likelihood (60%) approaches. Temporal analysis showed consistent construction land expansion alongside fluctuating cultivated land patterns, with other land types continuously declining.These findings demonstrate that integrating high-resolution remote sensing with Decision Tree classification significantly improves monitoring precision for non-agricultural land conversion, providing crucial scientific support for cropland conservation and sustainable land-use planning.

title: Dynamic distribution entropy analysis via ultrafast intracardiac echocardiography for monitoring of cardiac radiofrequency ablation
authors: Hyunhee Kim, Eunwoo Park, Joongho Ahn, Wonseok Choi, Chulhong Kim
abstract: Objective: To enable reliable, noise-robust monitoring of lesion formation during radiofrequency (RF) ablation with intracardiac echocardiography (ICE), this study introduces Dynamic Distribution Entropy (DDE), which quantifies temporal–spatial changes in backscatter statistics while suppressing ablation-induced interference. Methods: DDE integrates ultrafast plane-wave acquisition with singular value decomposition clutter filtering to stabilize entropy estimates. We evaluated DDE in (i) simulations modeling ablation-driven scatterer-size changes and (ii) ex-vivo porcine hearts imaged by ICE during RF ablation. DDE was compared with typical Shannon entropy, k-nearest neighbor entropy, cumulative residual entropy, and horizontally normalized Shannon entropy. Metrics included structural similarity (SSIM), intersection-over-union (IoU), and lesion-size agreement versus optical ground truth. Results: In simulations, DDE and CRE closely tracked dynamic scatterer evolution, yielding highest SSIM over frames (0.96). In ex-vivo experiments, DDE demonstrated the most accurate lesion-size estimation under RF-ON conditions, exhibiting the lowest bias (4.85 mm2), standard deviation (1.53 mm2), and root mean square error (5.08 mm2) among all evaluated entropy-based methods. Lesion sizes derived from DDE exhibited the best agreement with optical measurements. Conclusion: DDE provides robust and accurate intraoperative monitoring of lesion formation in ICE guided RF ablation, outperforming conventional entropy imaging under ablation-related noise. Significance: DDE offers a practical, noise-resistant quantitative ultrasound biomarker for real-time lesion assessment, supporting decision-making during ICE-guided cardiac ablation without requiring changes to clinical workflow.

title: An Intelligent Diagnosis Model for Elevator Brake Control Unit Faults Based on SPCA Feature Fusion
authors: Xudong Deng, Wei Huang, Naisha Wei
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computer Networks and Communications (SPCNC)
abstract: To address the complexity of elevator braking-system signals and improve diagnostic accuracy, this study designed an intelligent diagnosis model integrating a deep residual convolutional neural network (ResCNN) with a gradient-boosted decision tree (XGBoost). To achieve hierarchical fusion of multiscale features, an optimized residual architecture with a dualpathway architecture was developed. This study employed sparse principal component analysis (SPCA) for feature extraction from high-dimensional signals, which significantly improved the signal quality. The experimental results showed that the signal-to-noise ratio was improved by more than double. In the classification, the XGBoost algorithm was innovatively adopted to replace the traditional Softmax classifier. Combined with an improved loss function, this approach effectively mitigated the class imbalance issue. Experimental results showed that the model improved the diagnostic accuracy to $99.23 \%$ and reached an $\mathrm{F}-1$ score of $99.29 \%$. It outperformed existing models, with the recognition rate of earlystage faults considerably increased to $99.23 \%$. The research here provides a new solution to the intelligent operation and maintenance of electromechanical devices.

title: Feature Extraction of Digital Signal Constellation Diagram Based on Joint Blind Estimation
authors: Rui Wang, Jie Huang, Bingsong Xiao, Xing Xiang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computer Networks and Communications (SPCNC)
abstract: In response to the challenge of extracting digital signal constellation features caused by unknown carrier frequency and symbol rate parameters in non-cooperative communication, this paper proposes a constellation feature extraction method based on joint blind estimation. First, high-resolution spectral analysis is employed to achieve precise carrier frequency estimation. Subsequently, a multi-algorithm fusion strategy combined with confidence-weighted mechanism is introduced to complete symbol rate estimation, effectively mitigating errors induced by uncertain signal starting positions. To obtain more accurate constellation features, a matched filter optimal reception method based on early-late gate algorithm is adopted during symbol decision. Validation on typical modulation signals including 2ASK, 2FSK, BPSK, and QPSK demonstrates that the proposed method can reconstruct constellation diagrams highly consistent with theoretical models, confirming its universal applicability. Furthermore, experiments conducted under SNR conditions of $20 \mathrm{~dB}, 15 \mathrm{~dB}, 10 \mathrm{~dB}, 5 \mathrm{~dB}, 0 \mathrm{~dB}$ and -5 dB reveal stable recovery of clear constellation diagrams, indicating robust antiinterference capability. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed digital signal constellation feature extraction algorithm exhibits both universal applicability and strong anti-interference performance, providing foundational support for subsequent signal modulation recognition and parameter estimation.

title: Design and development of the RFID experimental platform based on Deepseek
authors: Xuan Zhong, Tiansong Li, Hang Liu, Hang Chai, Kang Li, Haozhuo Wang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Signal Processing, Computer Networks and Communications (SPCNC)
abstract: To meet the experimental teaching needs of radio frequency identification technology in universities, and to solve the problems of the traditional experimental platform’s single functionality, low visualization degree, and lack of mobile terminal interaction, this project has designed and implemented an RFID experimental teaching system that integrates a hardware experimental box, a Web simulation teaching platform, and an AI assistant. The experimental box is divided into two major modules: the principle machine and the application machine. The principle machine is based on a FPGA chip to implement modulation and demodulation as well as various encoding functions; the application machine uses Arduino UNO as the core to complete the reading and writing operations of ID cards in two frequency bands. Through local deployment of Deepseek, an RFID knowledge graph is constructed using the RAG architecture, and the AI assistant can immediately answer various questions such as students’ experimental operations, principle understanding, etc., achieving personalized tutoring. Meanwhile, the Web teaching platform adopts a B/S structure and embeds Simulink operation videos for ASK/FSK/PSK modulation and demodulation to help students understand the RFID circuit model and enhance learning effectiveness.

title: Design of High-Speed Front-End Readout Circuit for Photon Counting CT System
authors: Miao Li, Jun Liu, U-Fat Chio, Xi Wang, Jing Yang, Hongsheng Zhang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Frontiers Technology in Circuits and Systems (FTCS)
abstract: Based on the $0.11 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ CMOS process, a 64 channel photon counting high-speed front-end readout circuit was designed and implemented to meet the application requirements of the front-end chip of the photon counting cadmium zinc telluride detection system in high counting rate scenarios. This circuit integrates 64 parallel analog channels, bias modules, and logic control modules. The single channel innovatively adopts a synergistic design of charge sensitive amplifier and differential transconductance feedback reset structure, combined with leakage current compensation circuit, pole zero cancellation circuit, shaping filter, and fast peak detection and holding circuit to form a $0.1 \mathrm{fC} \sim 10 \mathrm{fC}$ large dynamic range high counting rate front-end signal processing link. Through theoretical modeling, the circuit architecture and performance characteristics of key modules were analyzed in detail, and their performance under high count rate conditions was comprehensively simulated. The simulation results show that within the input charge range of 0.1-10 fC (corresponding to energy of $2.9-290 \mathrm{keV}$), the system can achieve a photon counting rate of $\mathbf{1 0 ~ M}$-cps, a typical channel gain of $100 \mathrm{mV} / \mathrm{fC}$, an equivalent noise charge as low as $150 \mathrm{e}^{-}$, and a leakage current compensation capability of 1 nA. The key performance indicators of the circuit include a peak time of 40 ns, a nonlinearity of less than 1.2%, and an overall power consumption of only 350 mW, all of which meet the core technical requirements of the new generation photon counting CT system. This design achieves an excellent balance between high performance and low power consumption, providing a reliable solution for high-precision imaging applications.

title: A Calibration-Free Pipelined SAR ADC With Feedback Coefficient Compensation-Based Pipelined Residue Amplification Stage
authors: Jieming Liu, Dan Wang, Kexin Lu, Guohe Zhang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Frontiers Technology in Circuits and Systems (FTCS)
abstract: This paper proposes a feedback coefficient compensation-based pipelined residue amplification (RA) stage scheme. By compensating the feedback coefficient of the closedloop residue amplifier, this scheme reduces the design requirement for the open-loop gain of the residue amplifier compared with the traditional pipelined RA stage scheme. Meanwhile, this scheme doesn't require an additional calibration algorithm to calibrate the closed-loop gain of the residue amplifier and incurs minimal hardware overhead. Furthermore, since the traditional pipelined RA stage scheme requires additional reset timing, a high-speed successive approximation register (SAR) logic generation circuit is designed. This calibration-free 12-bit 1GS/s single-channel pipelined SAR analog-to-digital converter (ADC) is designed based on a $28-\mathrm{nm}$ CMOS process. At the Nyquist input frequency, it achieves a Spurious-Free Dynamic Range (SFDR) of 79.49 dB and a Signal-to-Noise and Distortion Ratio (SNDR) of 66.41 dB. It consumes 24.6 mW from a 0.9 V supply, with corresponding Schreier figure of merit (FoM) and Walden FoM of $\mathbf{1 6 9. 5 ~ d B}$ and $\mathbf{1 4. 4 ~ f J}$ /conv.-step, respectively.

title: Hybrid Framework with ResNet101 and RF-GBM Classification of MRI Brain Tumors
authors: Durga Devi A, Renganathan K
publish: 2025 IEEE First International Conference on Innovations in Engineering and Next-Generation Technologies for Sustainability (ICINVENTS)
abstract: A brain tumor classification based on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is essential in the initial diagnosis and treatment strategies. Common deep learning architectures tend to overfit and exhibit huge class imbalance with medical data. To address them, a hybrid ensemble is presented in this paper where the strong abilities of the Random Forest (RF) and the Gradient Boosting Machine (GBM) classifiers and the deep approach to feature extraction with ResNet101 are combined. ResNet101 is used to extract deep semantic features, classify which to find robustness and the better generalization, using the RF and GBM ensemble technique. Experiments were done on BRISC2025 dataset which consisted of four classes of brain MRI images glioma, meningioma, pituitary, and no tumor. The provided framework obtained results of 98.3% accuracy and the precision, recall, F1-scores were balanced across all categories. The confusion matrix proved the low number of misclassifications, and the training and validation curves revealed nice convergence without overfitting. Most importantly, recall on non-tumor cases was 100 percent, which meant that no healthy scans were false negatives in the framework. The comparison with the state-of-the-art based on comparative analysis demonstrates the competitiveness of the proposed hybrid solution in terms of accuracy corresponding to the high levels but also offering computational efficiency. These findings validate the idea to combine deep learning feature extraction and classical ensemble classifiers and build reliable and scalable brain tumor classification systems.

title: A Bayesian Neural Network Guided Fractional Order Level-Set Model for Segmenting Intensity Inhomogeneous Images
authors: Lin Zhu, Bingzhao Li
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Image Processing, Multimedia Technology and Machine Learning (IPMML)
abstract: Image segmentation in real world scenarios is strongly affected by noise, intensity inhomogeneity, and background complexity, making robust segmentation difficult. Traditional active contour models degrade under such conditions, while deep neural networks rely heavily on large annotated datasets and become unstable when training samples are limited or noisy. To address these issues, we propose a local active contour model that integrates Bayesian neural-network-guided feature representations with an adaptive fractional-order differential mechanism. The Bayesian module provides uncertainty-aware semantic cues, while the adaptive fractional-order term enhances robustness to local intensity variations. The combined model achieves improved stability, faster convergence, and greater resilience to noise and IIH. Experiments on synthetic, medical, and natural images demonstrate consistent improvements over existing approaches, highlighting the potential of integrating learnable representations with variational frameworks for challenging segmentation tasks.
